EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong thunderstorms affected portions of south-central and southeast Kansas during the afternoon hours on the 27th. The thunderstorms were slow movers, producing very heavy rainfall rates upwards of 1 to 2 inches per hour at times. Consequently, localized flooding occurred across mainly eastern portions of Sedgwick county, northeast portions of Sumner county and far western portions of Butler county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous strong to marginally severe thunderstorms raced east across eastern Kansas during the predawn hours on the 31st, along an advancing strong cold front. Nickel size hail was reported near Altamont in Labette county.

A strong upper level low pressure system moved from northern Old Mexico into New Mexico Wednesday night, January 27th, 2010.  This winter system then plowed east-northeast through Kansas, Texas and Oklahoma Thursday and early Friday, January 28th and 29th.  This brought significant snowfall to much of southwest and south central Kansas.  The precipitation began as light snow late Wednesday night, then picked up and continued as heavy snow for much of Thursday and Thursday night.  While snow persisted Friday morning, January 29th, in parts of our southeastern CWA, not much additional accumulation occurred.  Reports received Friday morning, January 29th, revealed a 5 to 20 mile wide swath of 7 to 11 inches of snowfall fell along an axis from near Hudson in northeastern Stafford County, to north of Haviland in Kiowa County, through northern parts of both Ford and Gray Counties, to just north of Big Bow in extreme northeastern Stanton County.  On either side of this heavy band of snow, an area of 4 to 6 inches of snow fell, basically south of a line from Shallow Water in southeast Scott County to Brownell in northeast Ness County to Pfeifer in southeast Ellis County, as well as north of a line from Elkhart in southeast Morton County to near Ashland in Clark county to near Sawyer in southeastern Pratt County.  In northern parts of DDC���s CWA, as well as in southeastern parts, 2 to 4 inches of snow fell.  The highest snowfall report received from this event was 11 inches at Ingalls in Gray County, with drifts as high as 5 feet in the Dodge City area also reported.  Winds during the event were from the north to northwest at 20 to 35 mph, which caused many areas of deep drifting and widespread travel problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 inches was reported in Larned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved across southern and central Kansas producing light to moderate snow over Greeley and Wichita counties.  Six inches of snow was reported in Leoti by local law enforcement, while four inches was reported in Tribune.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snow depth of six inches was measured in Leoti.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 6th and 7th 2010, a potent storm system moved across the region. This storm brought widespread snowfall of 3 to 6 inches to eastern Kansas and much of the northern half of Missouri. The storm system also brought bitterly cold temperatures, strong northwesterly winds and brutal wind chills to the area. Northwest winds of around 20 to 25 mph and gusts to around 35 mph were common across the region Thursday afternoon, creating significant blowing and drifting snow. These winds combined with temperatures in the single digits and lower teens, to produce wind chill values of -5 to -15 degrees during the daytime hours Thursday. ||The wind gradually decreased through Friday, as the storm system moved northeast of the region. Very cold temperatures overspread the area behind the storm system. Temperatures Friday morning ranged from 5 to -5 degrees, with Friday afternoon readings mainly in the 5 to 10 degree range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 5 inches of snow was reported across the county. Strong gusty northwest winds caused blowing and drifting snow.

This winter system then plowed east-northeast through Kansas, Texas and Oklahoma Thursday and early Friday, January 28th and 29th.  This brought significant snowfall to much of southwest and south central Kansas.  The precipitation began as light snow late Wednesday night, then picked up and continued as heavy snow for much of Thursday and Thursday night.  While snow persisted Friday morning, January 29th, in parts of our southeastern CWA, not much additional accumulation occurred.  Reports received Friday morning, January 29th, revealed a 5 to 20 mile wide swath of 7 to 11 inches of snowfall fell along an axis from near Hudson in northeastern Stafford County, to north of Haviland in Kiowa County, through northern parts of both Ford and Gray Counties, to just north of Big Bow in extreme northeastern Stanton County.  On either side of this heavy band of snow, an area of 4 to 6 inches of snow fell, basically south of a line from Shallow Water in southeast Scott County to Brownell in northeast Ness County to Pfeifer in southeast Ellis County, as well as north of a line from Elkhart in southeast Morton County to near Ashland in Clark county to near Sawyer in southeastern Pratt County.  In northern parts of DDC���s CWA, as well as in southeastern parts, 2 to 4 inches of snow fell.  The highest snowfall report received from this event was 11 inches at Ingalls in Gray County, with drifts as high as 5 feet in the Dodge City area also reported.  Winds during the event were from the north to northwest at 20 to 35 mph, which caused many areas of deep drifting and widespread travel problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 inches was reported in Scott City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving storm system tracked southeast across the Central Plains producing a swath of 3 to 6 inches of snow along with strong northwest winds. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 6th and 7th 2010, a potent storm system moved across the region. This storm brought widespread snowfall of 3 to 6 inches to eastern Kansas and much of the northern half of Missouri. The storm system also brought bitterly cold temperatures, strong northwesterly winds and brutal wind chills to the area. Northwest winds of around 20 to 25 mph and gusts to around 35 mph were common across the region Thursday afternoon, creating significant blowing and drifting snow. These winds combined with temperatures in the single digits and lower teens, to produce wind chill values of -5 to -15 degrees during the daytime hours Thursday. ||The wind gradually decreased through Friday, as the storm system moved northeast of the region. Very cold temperatures overspread the area behind the storm system. Temperatures Friday morning ranged from 5 to -5 degrees, with Friday afternoon readings mainly in the 5 to 10 degree range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 5 inches of snow was reported in Leavenworth. Strong gusty northwest winds caused blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early to mid-morning hours on the 18th, a thin layer of ice formed on area roads in and around the Wichita area, mainly on bridges and access ramps, causing more than two-dozen automobile accidents between midnight and 9 am. The thin layer of ice formed in response to below freezing temperatures interacting with wet roadways from rainfall the day before. Some minor injuries occurred from the automobile accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the early to mid-morning hours on the 18th, a thin layer of ice formed on area roads in and around the Wichita area, mainly on bridges and access ramps, causing more than two-dozen automobile accidents between midnight and 9 am. The thin layer of ice formed in response to below freezing temperatures interacting with wet roadways from rainfall the day before. Minor injuries occurred in four of the automobile accidents. The Wichita Eagle contributed to this report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south to southwest surface gradient winds developed during the morning hours and continued through most of the day across eastern Colorado, southwest Nebraska and western Kansas. Peak wind gusts of 50 to 70 mph were common across the area, producing minor damage, including a broken utility pole, and tree and awning damage.  One vehicle was blown over on Interstate 70 near Colby, Kansas but no injuries occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several other mesonet sites across Sherman county reported peak wind gusts to 60 mph. In Goodland, an elm tree was blown onto a roof and an awning was torn from a residence. A utility pole was snapped, resulting in a minor power outage in town.  A 40 foot ham radio antenna was also blown over.

A powerful, dynamic, and long duration winter storm, welcomed the arrival of Spring to the Missouri River Valley for March 19th through March 21st, 2010. An initial band of snow moved through the region, during the overnight hours of March 19 into the morning hours of Saturday March 20th, as an upper level storm system, started to develop in the Southern Plains. After a several hour pause in the snow early Saturday morning, additional precipitation developed across Kansas, Oklahoma, Arkansas, and southern Missouri, and then lifted northward into the area. This band of snow continued for 12 to 18 hours, as the upper level storm system drifted eastward, towards Arkansas and Louisiana by Saturday evening. The storm system finally moved far enough to the south by Sunday morning, to end the precipitation in the area. Snow totals upwards of 7 to 10 inches, was reported in areas generally along and southwest of the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to ten inches of snow was measured in Leavenworth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually cold Arctic airmass came across the area on January 6th and stayed through January 9th. This area has not seen a cold airmass like this since December 2005, and even February 1996 before that.  Besides the cold temperatures, this Arctic airmass brought in very strong winds throughout the area on the evening of January 6th and on Thursday, January the 7th.  The strong winds combined with the very cold temperatures creating dangerous wind chills. Wind chills through this cold outbreak created dangerous situations for people, animals, and property that were outdoors for a long period of time. Several schools across the area were cancelled or delayed, waterline mains bursted and slick driving conditions were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures in the single digits combined with wind chills of minus 15 to minus 20 degrees led to numerous water lines to burst across the county.  The city of Wichita water department reported 160 calls of burst water lines or burst water meters. The extreme wind chills also led to numerous schools in and around Wichita to cancel on Friday, January 8th, as minus 20 degree wind chills made it unsafe for children to wait at the bus stops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread dense fog affected much of central and south-central Kansas during the predawn hours on the 22nd. Visibilities were one-quarter mile or less. One truck accident was reported in Kingman county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Poor visibility in dense fog caused a trucker to drive his rig off the road and onto diagonally-oriented railroad tracks for one-quarter mile, causing damage to the tires and rims. No one was injured. The incident occurred about one mile west of Cunningham on Highway 54 westbound. Property damage amounts are rough estimates. The Kingman Leader-Courier contributed to this report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Thursday, February 25th, 2010, a cold front marched southeast through western Kansas, and produced a little snow over a small section of southwestern Kansas.  A narrow band of snow 3 to 5 inches in depth and from 5 to 15 miles wide, fell from near Liberal in Seward County northeastward to near Dodge City in Ford County.  A band of snow around 3 inches in depth and only 5 to 10 miles wide surrounded the 3 to 5 inch band.  Two inches or less fell south a line from Elkhart to Jetmore, and west of a line from Kinsley to Coldwater.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 4 inches was reported in Bucklin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front advanced southeast through southwest and south central Kansas during the day Sunday, February 21st.  This front ran into moisture flowing north from Oklahoma, resulting in snow, and a narrow band of ice, across much of south central Kansas.  By late Sunday morning, snow ranged from 3 inches in a narrow band from Larned to Greensburg, with 2 inches or less west of there but also east of a line from Wakeeney to Cimarron to extreme eastern Seward County.  Several car accidents were attributed to the icy, snowy road conditions, a couple of which were fatal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations of 1/4 were reported in St. John.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On May 12th 2010, a strong storm system affected the Southern and Central Plains.  In the National Weather Service Topeka Kansas County Warning Area (CWA), a warm front hovered east-west just to the south of Interstate 70 most of the day, while a cold front extended from Dickinson county into South Central Kansas.  Late in the afternoon, severe thunderstorms developed along the cold front, and moved northeastward across east central Kansas.  In the Topeka CWA, most reports of large hail and damaging winds occurred along or south of the Kansas Turnpike.  Elsewhere in the Topeka CWA, several storms produced hail 1/2 inch in diameter or smaller, and winds of 40 to 50 mph.||A National Weather Service storm survey team joined Douglas County Emergency Management in the field the afternoon of May 13th to assess wind damage in southern Douglas County.  A farmstead sustained damage to several sheds, wooden structures, trees, and a concrete silo.  Tin from the sheds was blown approximately 1/4 mile downstream.  Due to the unidirectional damage path, the survey team determined the damage had been caused by 75-80 mph straight line winds.

Thunderstorms moved into western Kansas from Colorado during the evening and slowly weakened as they moved east.  An isolated severe wind event occurred, possibly a heat burst given a ten degrees temperature rise, which caused damage at a local Walmart store in Colby.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain was also reported, including a brief power outage in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved into western Kansas from Colorado during the evening and slowly weakened as they moved east.  An isolated severe wind event occurred, possibly a heat burst given a ten degrees temperature rise, which caused damage at a local Walmart store in Colby.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind blown gravel was likely responsible for broken windows in four vehicles in a Walmart parking lot as a strong gust of wind developed from weakening thunderstorms. A door to an air conditioning building at the store was blown in, and the garden center was damaged.  In a nearby business several windows were broken in large agricultural vehicles.  A nearby weather station reported a wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On May 12th 2010, a strong storm system affected the Southern and Central Plains.  In the National Weather Service Topeka Kansas County Warning Area (CWA), a warm front hovered east-west just to the south of Interstate 70 most of the day, while a cold front extended from Dickinson county into South Central Kansas.  Late in the afternoon, severe thunderstorms developed along the cold front, and moved northeastward across east central Kansas.  In the Topeka CWA, most reports of large hail and damaging winds occurred along or south of the Kansas Turnpike.  Elsewhere in the Topeka CWA, several storms produced hail 1/2 inch in diameter or smaller, and winds of 40 to 50 mph.||A National Weather Service storm survey team joined Douglas County Emergency Management in the field the afternoon of May 13th to assess wind damage in southern Douglas County.  A farmstead sustained damage to several sheds, wooden structures, trees, and a concrete silo.  Tin from the sheds was blown approximately 1/4 mile downstream.  Due to the unidirectional damage path, the survey team determined the damage had been caused by 75-80 mph straight line winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of 6th Street and Tyler Street is impassable due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small cluster of storms with a bow echo, moved east across eastern Kansas and northern Missouri, during the morning hours of June 19, 2010. This storm system packed a strong punch, with radar estimated velocities topping out in the 80 to 90 knot range at times.  While the majority of this air speed didn't translate down to the surface, numerous reports of 60, to as high as 70 mph winds, were common from Troy to Kansas City.  This storm system remained intact long enough to cross the entire state of Missouri, making it a tri-state complex.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trailers were damaged by thunderstorm wind gusts, estimated up to 70 mph, near the Lake Contrary area.

A slow moving storm system brought heavy wet snow to much of Northeast and East Central Kansas over an extended period of time.  The snow began just before midnight on Friday March 19th across much of the area.  The snow was heavy at times during the night with thunder snow reported by several observers.  The snow decreased in coverage and intensity early Saturday morning when many areas had already received 3 to 6 inches on the 19th however it re-developed and produced another several inches of accumulation mainly across east central Kansas later Saturday into Saturday night.  By Sunday morning the 21st many areas along an Emporia to Topeka line had received 6 inches or more of wet snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine inches of snow was measured by the Emergency Manager in Garnett.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant low level moisture was in place across western Kansas February 3rd and 4th, 2010, when a strong upper level short wave crossed New Mexico and northern Oklahoma.  Snow began falling lightly across far western Kansas the evening of Wednesday, February 3rd.  The snow increased in intensity during Wednesday night and continued into Thursday, February 4th.  When finished, the snow had laid a swath of 5 to 6 inches of snowfall from Elkhart in Morton County to southern Meade County.  Three to four inches of snow fell south of a line from near Johnson in Stanton County to near Deerfield in Kearny County to western Barber County.  One to two inches of snow fell north of this line, with a one exception in western Trego County where 3 inches of snow fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow fell in Liberal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Thursday, February 25th, 2010, a cold front marched southeast through western Kansas, and produced a little snow over a small section of southwestern Kansas.  A narrow band of snow 3 to 5 inches in depth and from 5 to 15 miles wide, fell from near Liberal in Seward County northeastward to near Dodge City in Ford County.  A band of snow around 3 inches in depth and only 5 to 10 miles wide surrounded the 3 to 5 inch band.  Two inches or less fell south a line from Elkhart to Jetmore, and west of a line from Kinsley to Coldwater.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 4 inches was reported 17 miles southwest of Meade.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful, dynamic, and long duration winter storm, welcomed the arrival of Spring to the Missouri River Valley for March 19th through March 21st, 2010. An initial band of snow moved through the region, during the overnight hours of March 19 into the morning hours of Saturday March 20th, as an upper level storm system, started to develop in the Southern Plains. After a several hour pause in the snow early Saturday morning, additional precipitation developed across Kansas, Oklahoma, Arkansas, and southern Missouri, and then lifted northward into the area. This band of snow continued for 12 to 18 hours, as the upper level storm system drifted eastward, towards Arkansas and Louisiana by Saturday evening. The storm system finally moved far enough to the south by Sunday morning, to end the precipitation in the area. Snow totals upwards of 7 to 10 inches, was reported in areas generally along and southwest of the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven inches of snow was measured in Troy.

The snow began just before midnight on Friday March 19th across much of the area.  The snow was heavy at times during the night with thunder snow reported by several observers.  The snow decreased in coverage and intensity early Saturday morning when many areas had already received 3 to 6 inches on the 19th however it re-developed and produced another several inches of accumulation mainly across east central Kansas later Saturday into Saturday night.  By Sunday morning the 21st many areas along an Emporia to Topeka line had received 6 inches or more of wet snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured 8 inches of snow storm total since it began late Friday night in Eudora.  Some melting had occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful, dynamic, and long duration winter storm, welcomed the arrival of Spring to the Missouri River Valley for March 19th through March 21st, 2010. An initial band of snow moved through the region, during the overnight hours of March 19 into the morning hours of Saturday March 20th, as an upper level storm system, started to develop in the Southern Plains. After a several hour pause in the snow early Saturday morning, additional precipitation developed across Kansas, Oklahoma, Arkansas, and southern Missouri, and then lifted northward into the area. This band of snow continued for 12 to 18 hours, as the upper level storm system drifted eastward, towards Arkansas and Louisiana by Saturday evening. The storm system finally moved far enough to the south by Sunday morning, to end the precipitation in the area. Snow totals upwards of 7 to 10 inches, was reported in areas generally along and southwest of the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of snow was measured in Bonner Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms first developed during the afternoon of June 8th, 2010, along a cold front that was pushing steadily south across Kansas. Additional storms formed north of the cold front as the overnight hours progressed into the early morning of June 9th, 2010, providing several rounds of storms for locations generally along and southeast of the Kansas Turnpike.  Many of these storms quickly became severe with wet microburst winds. However, with an extremely moist air mass in place all of the storms produced very high rainfall rates that caused widespread flooding across portions of South Central and especially Southeast Kansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms first developed during the afternoon of June 8th, 2010, along a cold front that was pushing steadily south across Kansas. Additional storms formed north of the cold front as the overnight hours progressed into the early morning of June 9th, 2010, providing several rounds of storms for locations generally along and southeast of the Kansas Turnpike.  Many of these storms quickly became severe with wet microburst winds. However, with an extremely moist air mass in place all of the storms produced very high rainfall rates that caused widespread flooding across portions of South Central and especially Southeast Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall since midnight was 2.20 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Oppressive heat and humidity for June standards affected a small portion of southeast Kansas during the afternoon hours on the 27th. Heat indices from 103 to 107 degrees were common. No reports of heat related illnesses, deaths, property or crop damages were received. A strong cold front moved southeast across the area late on the 27th, ushering cooler air southward across the area.

On the afternoon of April 6th, 2010 a line of storms formed along a dry line  in central Kansas.  These storms produced large hail up to golfball sized and and damaging winds near 70 mph across the area. By 4:00 pm the line of storms moved into east central Kansas and affected Lawrence.  Severe winds ripped through the northern portions of town causing significant damage to some of the neighborhoods along and north of 6th Street.  The Lawrence Airport ASOS reported a wind gust of 68 mph at 4:39 pm.  No structures were reported as being damaged, however, many trees greater than 24 inches in diameter were snapped as a result of the strong winds.  After the winds left the area heavy rain affected Lawrence for a couple hours thereafter and caused minor street flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of the previous day's account.  On the afternoon of April 29th, 2010 strong to severe thunderstorms formed across northern Kansas bringing large hail to the size of golfballs and damaging winds over 60 mph. A brief tornado touchdown occurred over open country approximately 4 miles northeast of Washington.  No condensation funnel appeared to reach the ground however dust was noted beneath the rotating wall cloud indicating a ground circulation on two occasions for a period of about 30 seconds.  No damage was found to have occurred.  The tornado was documented by the general public and storm chasers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emporia ASOS measured a 59 mph wind gust associated with strong gradient winds.

Severe winds ripped through the northern portions of town causing significant damage to some of the neighborhoods along and north of 6th Street.  The Lawrence Airport ASOS reported a wind gust of 68 mph at 4:39 pm.  No structures were reported as being damaged, however, many trees greater than 24 inches in diameter were snapped as a result of the strong winds.  After the winds left the area heavy rain affected Lawrence for a couple hours thereafter and caused minor street flooding.

A weak disturbance moved across South Central Kansas during the afternoon of August 10th, 2010.  As this disturbance encountered a very warm and humid airmass, showers and thunderstorms developed across Central and South Central Kansas. One of the thunderstorms produced some isolated damaging wind gusts to 70 mph.

The combination of temperatures around 100 degrees and dewpoints in the low to mid 70s combined to produce oppressive heat and humidity during the afternoon and early evening hours of August 1st through August 4th. The warm temperatures topped out at 109 degrees on August 3rd. Heat indices ranged from 105 to 115 degrees, mainly over south-central and southeast Kansas, but also over southern portions of central and east-central Kansas. The only relief from the heat was a stout southerly wind.

Beginning in the late afternoon hours of Thursday, a batch of showers and thunderstorms developed in Ottawa and Cloud Counties behind a cold front.  At the time, the storms were not severe, but as they moved east southeast, winds in excess of 60 mph and hail to the size of quarters were reported.||Storm reports consisted of quarter-sized hail near the towns of Eskridge, Carbondale, and Scranton in Wabaunsee and Osage Counties respectively.  As the storm moved over southern Wabaunsee County at 4:00 PM, a localized microburst with estimated winds in excess of 70 mph caused extensive damage to trees, power lines, and small structures.  One report from the town of Chalk in Wabaunsee County had two-foot diameter trees uprooted, bales of hay blown apart, power poles downed, and a dome on top of a silo collapsed.

Strong thunderstorms brought heavy rain to much of north central, northeast, and east central Kansas on Tuesday night and Wednesday.  Thunderstorms moved across northern Kansas Tuesday night into Wednesday morning with very heavy rainfall.  For the remainder of the afternoon and evening, thunderstorm development tended to train over the same areas for 1 to 3 hours.  Spotters reported rainfall rates in the range of 1 to 2 inches per hour, with numerous rainfall totals in the 3 to 6 inch range.  This did cause flash flooding across the region...with Anderson and southern Franklin counties receiving the most widespread flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported 2 inches of flowing water over 1350 Avenue and Union Road near Woodbine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of September 18, 2010 very strong thunderstorms formed along two colliding boundaries in northeast Kansas. At least two of these storms morphed into supercells and dropped hail the size of baseballs and larger on portions of Jefferson County. Elsewhere, these storms produced hail up to the size of golf balls. A few isolated reports of strong damaging winds were reported, however most of the severe weather reports from September 18th were large hail reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported water over the road at K4 and 138th Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms brought heavy rain to much of north central, northeast, and east central Kansas on Tuesday night and Wednesday.  Thunderstorms moved across northern Kansas Tuesday night into Wednesday morning with very heavy rainfall.  For the remainder of the afternoon and evening, thunderstorm development tended to train over the same areas for 1 to 3 hours.  Spotters reported rainfall rates in the range of 1 to 2 inches per hour, with numerous rainfall totals in the 3 to 6 inch range.  This did cause flash flooding across the region...with Anderson and southern Franklin counties receiving the most widespread flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager from Anderson County reported flash flooding across numerous rural roads near Garnett.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of Friday September 10, 2010 a cold front pushed through northeast Kansas, and kicked off a round of thunderstorms which brought strong damaging winds to areas near Topeka.  Trained spotters around the area reported wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph. These wind gusts caused tree limbs up to 10 inches in diameter to break.  The Automated Surface Observation System (ASOS) site at Emporia measured a 68 mph wind gust, while the ASOS station at Forbes Field, south of Topeka, measured a gust of 55 mph. Aside from the widespread strong winds the storms produced large hail up to the size of golf balls in isolated locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emporia Automated Surface Observing System (ASOS) measured two wind gusts of 68 mph.

On the afternoon of September 13, 2010 another round of severe thunderstorms kicked off along a surface boundary in northeast Kansas.  Some of the storms became severe with strong damaging winds and large hail to the size of golf balls.  Although winds gusts of up to 70 mph were reported to the National Weather Service in Topeka, no significant damage was reported. Heavy rain was also reported from the storms as 2 to 3 inches fell over a short period of time across north central Kansas.  By Monday night, the storms formed into a squall line and moved eastward into the Kansas City metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was flowing across Highway 77, 1 mile south of Marysville. City Park in Marysville is also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning in the late afternoon hours of Thursday, a batch of showers and thunderstorms developed in Ottawa and Cloud Counties behind a cold front.  At the time, the storms were not severe, but as they moved east southeast, winds in excess of 60 mph and hail to the size of quarters were reported.||Storm reports consisted of quarter-sized hail near the towns of Eskridge, Carbondale, and Scranton in Wabaunsee and Osage Counties respectively. On the afternoon of April 6th, 2010 a line of storms formed along a dry line  in central Kansas.  These storms produced large hail up to golfball sized and and damaging winds near 70 mph across the area. By 4:00 pm the line of storms moved into east central Kansas and affected Lawrence.  Severe winds ripped through the northern portions of town causing significant damage to some of the neighborhoods along and north of 6th Street.  The Lawrence Airport ASOS reported a wind gust of 68 mph at 4:39 pm.  No structures were reported as being damaged, however, many trees greater than 24 inches in diameter were snapped as a result of the strong winds.  After the winds left the area heavy rain affected Lawrence for a couple hours thereafter and caused minor street flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of temperatures around 100 degrees and dewpoints in the low to mid 70s combined to produce oppressive heat and humidity during the afternoon and early evening hours of August 1st through August 4th. The warm temperatures topped out at 109 degrees on August 3rd. Heat indices ranged from 105 to 115 degrees, mainly over south-central and southeast Kansas, but also over southern portions of central and east-central Kansas. The only relief from the heat was a stout southerly wind.

Strong thunderstorms brought heavy rain to much of north central, northeast, and east central Kansas on Tuesday night and Wednesday.  Thunderstorms moved across northern Kansas Tuesday night into Wednesday morning with very heavy rainfall.  For the remainder of the afternoon and evening, thunderstorm development tended to train over the same areas for 1 to 3 hours.  Spotters reported rainfall rates in the range of 1 to 2 inches per hour, with numerous rainfall totals in the 3 to 6 inch range.  This did cause flash flooding across the region...with Anderson and southern Franklin counties receiving the most widespread flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 31 near Harris was flooded with 8 to 9 inches of running water.  Also several county roads flooded near Richmond.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms, associated with a cold front, pushed across southeast Kansas and into Missouri. The storms initially produced severe wind gusts up to 75 mph in Kansas, then produced flooding rains across portions of central Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of 60 mph winds were received by the office of Emergency Management. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Wednesday thunderstorms formed near mid day in Clay, Geary, Riley, and Pottawatomie Counties.  Excessive rainfall in the area was caused by many storms producing heavy rain passing over the same area.  Heavy rain during the previous day caused the ground to become saturated.  During the afternoon of June 16th nearly 2 more inches fell across this area in a short period of time causing flash flooding.  As the storm system moved eastward it continued to produce heavy rain.  As it went through Topeka it dropped 2.31 inches of rain in one hour and an additional .45 inch the next hour for a total of 2.76 inches over the two hour span between 5 pm and 7 pm.  By the time the rain stopped 2.78 inches of rain fell at Billard Airport which broke the one day record rainfall total for June 16th.  The record was previously held by the year 1996.   Many cars stalled in flooded streets in Topeka as the water rose.  In Lawrence heavy rain caused significant flooding to streets and parks within the city.  At one point the Buford M. Watson park at Tennessee St and 7th St. near downtown Lawrence was under eight feet of rushing water.  Numerous water rescues were conducted throughout town as motorists became stranded by the quickly rising water.  Aside from Topeka and Lawrence, the hardest hit areas from the deluge were portions of Pottawatomie and Riley Counties as heavy rain caused many roads to be closed.||Flooding was not the only type of hazardous weather to impact northeastern Kansas on June 16.  Some of the storms responsible for the flash flooding also brought severe hail and wind to the area.  Many reports of quarter sized hail were received by the National Weather Service office in Topeka, and a storm near Junction City brought golf ball sized hail to that area around 2 PM.  Strong, damaging winds associated with the  storms also affected portions of Shawnee and Douglas Counties.  The NWS in Topeka observed a 62 mph wind gust, and an automated weather station at Lawrence Municipal Airport reported a 58 mph wind gust.  As a result of the strong winds many tree limbs up to 6 inches in diameter snapped off of trees in Topeka and Lawrence.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous County Roads were flooded due to measured rainfall of 2.30 inches in approximately 30 to 40 minutes.

A slow moving cold front moved into an extremely hot and unstable airmass across central and eastern Kansas on Friday, August 13th, and sparked thunderstorms along a line from Salina to Seneca.  The airmass feeding into these storms was extremely unstable, with temperatures in the 100 to 106 degree range and dewpoint temperatures in the 60s and 70s.  This led to rapid storm intensification, with the first severe thunderstorm warning being issued less than 30 minutes after the storms formed. ||The Manhattan airport tower measured a wind gust of 93 mph at the peak of the storm, with similar wind speeds in the 70 to 95 mph range from southern Manhattan through Ogden and other parts of southern Riley county.  The storm complex tracked eastward across southern Pottawatomie, northern Wabaunsee, southern Jackson, northern Shawnee and Jefferson counties.  While it weakened a bit as it pushed east, the storms left a swath of wind damage.||Additional severe thunderstorms developed along the front to the northeast and southwest of the initial complex, bringing repeated rounds of damaging winds of 50 to 70 mph, a few reports of hail larger than quarters, and intense cloud to ground lightning.  As the event wore on into the evening hours, thunderstorms continued to produce wind damage, with a truck being blown off the road and power lines down in Lyon county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At a residence 5 miles west of Junction City a metal outbuilding was destroyed, and debris was found nearly a quarter of a mile downwind.  Closer to Junction City, a gas station lost part of its awning.  Trees and power lines were reported down across Junction City resulting in many damaged roofs and cars and widespread power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system aloft moved across the area during the afternoon hours.  At the same time a surface dry line lingered over western Kansas before moving rapidly eastward, followed by a cold front.  Strong thunderstorms developed by early afternoon on these surface features.  In eastern Colorado, several severe hail reports were received during the late afternoon.  In western Kansas, numerous tornadoes developed along the dry line just after noon, with one destroying a small shed.

Heavy rain fell overnight across Gove county with cooperative observers reporting 2-3 inches of rain.  Unofficial reports of 5-9 inches were received from county employees 8-10 miles south of Gove City.  Minor field flooding and full ditches were observed during the morning.  Additional heavy rainfall during the afternoon resulted in flooding in local areas of the county especially from the city of Gove to about 10 miles south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of water was reported running over streets in the city of Gove with many reports of ponding elsewhere in the county, especially south of Gove city.

A slow moving cold front moved into an extremely hot and unstable airmass across central and eastern Kansas on Friday, August 13th, and sparked thunderstorms along a line from Salina to Seneca.  The airmass feeding into these storms was extremely unstable, with temperatures in the 100 to 106 degree range and dewpoint temperatures in the 60s and 70s.  This led to rapid storm intensification, with the first severe thunderstorm warning being issued less than 30 minutes after the storms formed. ||The Manhattan airport tower measured a wind gust of 93 mph at the peak of the storm, with similar wind speeds in the 70 to 95 mph range from southern Manhattan through Ogden and other parts of southern Riley county.  The storm complex tracked eastward across southern Pottawatomie, northern Wabaunsee, southern Jackson, northern Shawnee and Jefferson counties.  While it weakened a bit as it pushed east, the storms left a swath of wind damage.||Additional severe thunderstorms developed along the front to the northeast and southwest of the initial complex, bringing repeated rounds of damaging winds of 50 to 70 mph, a few reports of hail larger than quarters, and intense cloud to ground lightning.  As the event wore on into the evening hours, thunderstorms continued to produce wind damage, with a truck being blown off the road and power lines down in Lyon county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree 24 inches in the diameter was reported uprooted on the west side of Highway 77 near Geary County Lake.  Time of the report was estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several slow-moving supercells developed in extreme eastern Colorado and moved across Greeley, Wichita, Logan and Gove counties, producing five tornadoes and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report relayed via Vortex 2 field project.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Wednesday thunderstorms formed near mid day in Clay, Geary, Riley, and Pottawatomie Counties.  Excessive rainfall in the area was caused by many storms producing heavy rain passing over the same area.  Heavy rain during the previous day caused the ground to become saturated.  During the afternoon of June 16th nearly 2 more inches fell across this area in a short period of time causing flash flooding.  As the storm system moved eastward it continued to produce heavy rain.  As it went through Topeka it dropped 2.31 inches of rain in one hour and an additional .45 inch the next hour for a total of 2.76 inches over the two hour span between 5 pm and 7 pm.  By the time the rain stopped 2.78 inches of rain fell at Billard Airport which broke the one day record rainfall total for June 16th.  The record was previously held by the year 1996.   Many cars stalled in flooded streets in Topeka as the water rose.  In Lawrence heavy rain caused significant flooding to streets and parks within the city.  At one point the Buford M. Watson park at Tennessee St and 7th St. near downtown Lawrence was under eight feet of rushing water.  Numerous water rescues were conducted throughout town as motorists became stranded by the quickly rising water.  Aside from Topeka and Lawrence, the hardest hit areas from the deluge were portions of Pottawatomie and Riley Counties as heavy rain caused many roads to be closed.||Flooding was not the only type of hazardous weather to impact northeastern Kansas on June 16.  Some of the storms responsible for the flash flooding also brought severe hail and wind to the area.  Many reports of quarter sized hail were received by the National Weather Service office in Topeka, and a storm near Junction City brought golf ball sized hail to that area around 2 PM.  Strong, damaging winds associated with the  storms also affected portions of Shawnee and Douglas Counties.  The NWS in Topeka observed a 62 mph wind gust, and an automated weather station at Lawrence Municipal Airport reported a 58 mph wind gust.  As a result of the strong winds many tree limbs up to 6 inches in diameter snapped off of trees in Topeka and Lawrence.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 99 near mile marker 156 closed due to water running over the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Wednesday thunderstorms formed near mid day in Clay, Geary, Riley, and Pottawatomie Counties.  Excessive rainfall in the area was caused by many storms producing heavy rain passing over the same area.  Heavy rain during the previous day caused the ground to become saturated.  During the afternoon of June 16th nearly 2 more inches fell across this area in a short period of time causing flash flooding.  As the storm system moved eastward it continued to produce heavy rain.  As it went through Topeka it dropped 2.31 inches of rain in one hour and an additional .45 inch the next hour for a total of 2.76 inches over the two hour span between 5 pm and 7 pm.  By the time the rain stopped 2.78 inches of rain fell at Billard Airport which broke the one day record rainfall total for June 16th.  The record was previously held by the year 1996.   Many cars stalled in flooded streets in Topeka as the water rose.  In Lawrence heavy rain caused significant flooding to streets and parks within the city.  At one point the Buford M. Watson park at Tennessee St and 7th St. near downtown Lawrence was under eight feet of rushing water.  Numerous water rescues were conducted throughout town as motorists became stranded by the quickly rising water.  Aside from Topeka and Lawrence, the hardest hit areas from the deluge were portions of Pottawatomie and Riley Counties as heavy rain caused many roads to be closed.||Flooding was not the only type of hazardous weather to impact northeastern Kansas on June 16.  Some of the storms responsible for the flash flooding also brought severe hail and wind to the area.  Many reports of quarter sized hail were received by the National Weather Service office in Topeka, and a storm near Junction City brought golf ball sized hail to that area around 2 PM.  Strong, damaging winds associated with the  storms also affected portions of Shawnee and Douglas Counties.  The NWS in Topeka observed a 62 mph wind gust, and an automated weather station at Lawrence Municipal Airport reported a 58 mph wind gust.  As a result of the strong winds many tree limbs up to 6 inches in diameter snapped off of trees in Topeka and Lawrence.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were reported flooded between Manhattan, Keats, and Ogden. Fort Riley Blvd and Wildcat Creek near Manhattan Airport closed. Anderson Avenue near Keats was also closed due to high water running over the road.

An upper level disturbance approaching from the southwest in concert with increasing low-level moisture resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms across southeast Kansas during the evening hours on the 22nd. One-inch hail and 50 mph winds was reported in the Cedar Vale area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated at 50 mph occurred with the hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several clusters of intense thunderstorms moved north across the area during the late afternoon and evening hours, producing several tornadoes in northwest Kansas.  Other areas nearby received hail up to the size of golf balls and wind gusts to 80 mph.

A slow moving cold front moved into an extremely hot and unstable airmass across central and eastern Kansas on Friday, August 13th, and sparked thunderstorms along a line from Salina to Seneca.  The airmass feeding into these storms was extremely unstable, with temperatures in the 100 to 106 degree range and dewpoint temperatures in the 60s and 70s.  This led to rapid storm intensification, with the first severe thunderstorm warning being issued less than 30 minutes after the storms formed. ||The Manhattan airport tower measured a wind gust of 93 mph at the peak of the storm, with similar wind speeds in the 70 to 95 mph range from southern Manhattan through Ogden and other parts of southern Riley county.  The storm complex tracked eastward across southern Pottawatomie, northern Wabaunsee, southern Jackson, northern Shawnee and Jefferson counties.  While it weakened a bit as it pushed east, the storms left a swath of wind damage.||Additional severe thunderstorms developed along the front to the northeast and southwest of the initial complex, bringing repeated rounds of damaging winds of 50 to 70 mph, a few reports of hail larger than quarters, and intense cloud to ground lightning.  As the event wore on into the evening hours, thunderstorms continued to produce wind damage, with a truck being blown off the road and power lines down in Lyon county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported blown down near Parkerville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of September 13, 2010 another round of severe thunderstorms kicked off along a surface boundary in northeast Kansas.  Some of the storms became severe with strong damaging winds and large hail to the size of golf balls.  Although winds gusts of up to 70 mph were reported to the National Weather Service in Topeka, no significant damage was reported. Heavy rain was also reported from the storms as 2 to 3 inches fell over a short period of time across north central Kansas.  By Monday night, the storms formed into a squall line and moved eastward into the Kansas City metro area.

On the afternoon of September 18, 2010 very strong thunderstorms formed along two colliding boundaries in northeast Kansas. At least two of these storms morphed into supercells and dropped hail the size of baseballs and larger on portions of Jefferson County. Elsewhere, these storms produced hail up to the size of golf balls. A few isolated reports of strong damaging winds were reported, however most of the severe weather reports from September 18th were large hail reports.

A cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms developed over parts of northeast Kansas on Tuesday afternoon, and preceded to drop copious amounts of rainfall over a relatively small area.  Radar estimations of total rainfall from the storm system were as high as 6 to 8 inches over parts of Nemaha, Brown, and Jackson Counties, while one report of 9.25 inches of rain was received.  Much of this rain fell in 2 to 3 hours.  Flash flooding was reported across the warned area with several road closures taking place where creeks and streams were flowing out of their banks and crossing country roads and a few state highways.  In addition to the flash flooding, hail up to the size of quarters was also reported with these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The County emergency manager reported 6 to 12 inches of water running over Highway 187, 2 miles south Highway 36.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening hours of May  10th, 2010, ahead of an approaching dry line and warm front.  This dynamic environment coupled with an unstable airmass led to the development of thunderstorms to the west and south of Wichita, Kansas with tornado producing supercells moving across portions of South Central Kansas. Two supercells in particular produced significant damage across portions of Kingman, Sedgwick and Cowley counties with some of the damage classified as EF-2 damage by survey teams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two semi tractor trailers were blown off of the Kansas Turnpike right at the state line as the tornado crossed the interstate. The overturned semi's temporarily closed Interstate 35 for a short time as they blocked traffic in both directions.  The tornado also caused significant tree damage as it moved northeast into rural Sumner County, Kansas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several tree limbs were reported blocking part of the highway.

Some of the storms became severe with strong damaging winds and large hail to the size of golf balls.  Although winds gusts of up to 70 mph were reported to the National Weather Service in Topeka, no significant damage was reported. Heavy rain was also reported from the storms as 2 to 3 inches fell over a short period of time across north central Kansas.  By Monday night, the storms formed into a squall line and moved eastward into the Kansas City metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter near Robinson reported a 60 mph wind gust.

A cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms developed over parts of northeast Kansas on Tuesday afternoon, and preceded to drop copious amounts of rainfall over a relatively small area.  Radar estimations of total rainfall from the storm system were as high as 6 to 8 inches over parts of Nemaha, Brown, and Jackson Counties, while one report of 9.25 inches of rain was received.  Much of this rain fell in 2 to 3 hours.  Flash flooding was reported across the warned area with several road closures taking place where creeks and streams were flowing out of their banks and crossing country roads and a few state highways.  In addition to the flash flooding, hail up to the size of quarters was also reported with these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported water flowing over Kestrel Road, south of 170th Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning started a grass fire which consumed 500 acres of farmland west of Atwood.  No loss of buildings or livestock was reported.  Strong winds fanned the fire making it difficult to control.  Five separate fire crews eventually responded to the scene.  One firefighter was treated for smoke inhalation and heat exhaustion.  Another smaller fire, also ignited by lightning, consumed 60 acres near Ludell, but it was easily contained.

Patchy light freezing rain and freezing drizzle during the evening hours on the 23rd produced a thin glaze of ice across central, south-central and portions of southeast Kansas. The glaze of ice produced very slick roads through the early morning hours on the 24th. Several automobile accidents and slide-offs occurred as a result, producing numerous injuries. One fatality occurred in Cowley county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periodic light freezing drizzle and light freezing rain created a thin glaze of ice across the area, resulting in at least 20 traffic accidents and slide-offs countywide. At least 4 injuries were reported. Property damage amounts are rough estimates. The Newton Kansan contributed to this report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Patchy light freezing rain and freezing drizzle during the evening hours on the 23rd produced a thin glaze of ice across central, south-central and portions of southeast Kansas. The glaze of ice produced very slick roads through the early morning hours on the 24th. Several automobile accidents and slide-offs occurred as a result, producing numerous injuries. One fatality occurred in Cowley county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periodic light freezing drizzle and light freezing rain created a thin glaze of ice across the area, resulting in numerous traffic accidents countywide. Six relatively minor injuries were reported. Unfortunately, one fatality occurred when a 60-year-old man lost control of his vehicle on the icy roads. Property damage amounts are rough estimates. The Winfield Daily Courier contributed to this report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a remarkably snow-free start to winter, the last day of 2010 finally brought the first widespread accumulations of the season to North Central Kansas, as a powerful upper level low pressure system tracked along a path from northeast Colorado to southeast South Dakota.  The main event impacted the area during the pre-dawn and daytime hours on Friday, New Year's Eve, with accumulating snow ending in most places by early afternoon. Storm total snowfall across most of North Central Kansas was fairly uniform, with most spots only tallying between 1-3 inches. Due to the fact that the main part of the storm tracked west-northwest of the area, the heaviest snow, strongest winds, and worst visibilities focused across northern and western Nebraska into the Dakotas, where blizzard conditions were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports from the county ranged from 1 to 3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions combined with winds gusting to 30mph and low relative humidity to allow a wild fire near Galena, Kansas to burn out of control for several hours. The fire burned around 100 acres of off Schermerhorn Road and destroyed a couple of mobile homes. One resident was injured and taken to the hospital for smoke inhalation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 100 acre wildfire destroyed several mobile homes and injured one resident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface pressure gradient across the region produced gusty northwest winds for much of the day.  Wind gusts of 40 to 60 mph caused minor sign and roof damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periodic light freezing drizzle and light freezing rain created a thin glaze of ice across the area, resulting in dozens of traffic accidents countywide, including a 14-vehicle pileup in Salina. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Patchy light freezing rain and freezing drizzle during the evening hours on the 23rd produced a thin glaze of ice across central, south-central and portions of southeast Kansas. The glaze of ice produced very slick roads through the early morning hours on the 24th. Several automobile accidents and slide-offs occurred as a result, producing numerous injuries. One fatality occurred in Cowley county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periodic light freezing drizzle and light freezing rain created a thin glaze of ice across the area, resulting in at least 10 traffic accidents countywide according to law enforcement. Only one injury was reported due to the traffic accidents. Property damage amounts are rough estimates. The Peabody Gazette-Bulletin and the Marion County Record contributed to this report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snow to southeast Kansas and southern Missouri on Friday, January 29, 2010.  Light snow began the evening of January 28th then increased in intensity on January 29th as a storm system tracked across Arkansas.  Total storm snow accumulations of 5 to 8 inches were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow accumulations of 5 to 8 inches. Sleet accumulations up to one half inch before the snow began.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm affected the region from the late afternoon hours on the 28th through the late evening hours on the 29th. Precipitation type was predominately snow. Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 8 inches for most locations, with pockets of 1 to 3 inch amounts, especially across south-central Kansas. Portions of central Kansas received the lowest totals, averaging only 1 to 2 inches in spots. Stout northerly winds produced areas of blowing and drifting snow. Numerous school districts canceled classes and activities for the 29th. Fortunately, no injury accidents were reported throughout the event, although a handful of relatively minor non-injury auto accidents did occur across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations ranged from 5 to 7 inches across southeast portions of the county, to 2 to 5 inches elsewhere across the county. The lowest amounts were found across northern portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually cold Arctic airmass came across the area on January 6th and stayed through January 9th. This area has not seen a cold airmass like this since December 2005, and even February 1996 before that.  Besides the cold temperatures, this Arctic airmass brought in very strong winds throughout the area on the evening of January 6th and on Thursday, January the 7th.  The strong winds combined with the very cold temperatures creating dangerous wind chills. Wind chills through this cold outbreak created dangerous situations for people, animals, and property that were outdoors for a long period of time. Several schools across the area were cancelled or delayed, waterline mains bursted and slick driving conditions were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight temperatures dropped to zero in Kingman.  This caused pipes to rupture at the VFW hall in Kingman.  The heat was inadvertently turned off which led to the pipes freezing.  The resulting pipes in the ceiling burst, cascading water into the kitchen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another winter storm, in an already active winter season, impacted much of Kansas and Missouri, during the weekend of February 20-21, 2010. This challenging winter storm, brought numerous forecast problems with hazards ranging from heavy snow, to sleet, ice, rain, and even flooding. This upper level storm system originated well off the California coast. Its general track was typical of a classic El Nino pattern, moving across the Southwest and lifting northeast into the Missouri, and eventually the Ohio River Valley regions. At the surface, several waves of precipitation moved through the area, with periodic sleet, snow, and rain showers, persisting from late Saturday afternoon through daybreak Sunday. However, the heaviest precipitation was still to come. ||Late on the 21st, a large area of thunderstorms developed in Texas, Oklahoma, and Kansas and began streaming northeast towards the area. While thunderstorm activity diminished as precipitation arrived, heavy rainfall still took place across portions of central Missouri. Numerous streams, rivers, and creeks went above flood stage. This precipitation area also produced rapid ice accumulations, south of the Missouri River. ||However, this was only the beginning of the heavy precipitation. As an area of surface low pressure and upper level low passed just south of the area, a large but incredibly intense band of snowfall, developed in areas of northern Kansas and northern Missouri on the morning of the 21st. This band of snow persisted through the evening hours of the 21st, producing snowfall rates between 1 and 2 inches per hour at times. Snowfall tapered off by the 22nd, as the upper level storm system moved well east of the region. Snowfall totals reached a foot in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mixture of snow, sleet, and freezing rain, caused hazardous driving conditions across the area. Ice accumulations reached a quarter of an inch in spots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system moved from northern Old Mexico into New Mexico Wednesday night, January 27th, 2010.  This winter system then plowed east-northeast through Kansas, Texas and Oklahoma Thursday and early Friday, January 28th and 29th.  This brought significant snowfall to much of southwest and south central Kansas.  The precipitation began as light snow late Wednesday night, then picked up and continued as heavy snow for much of Thursday and Thursday night.  While snow persisted Friday morning, January 29th, in parts of our southeastern CWA, not much additional accumulation occurred.  Reports received Friday morning, January 29th, revealed a 5 to 20 mile wide swath of 7 to 11 inches of snowfall fell along an axis from near Hudson in northeastern Stafford County, to north of Haviland in Kiowa County, through northern parts of both Ford and Gray Counties, to just north of Big Bow in extreme northeastern Stanton County.  On either side of this heavy band of snow, an area of 4 to 6 inches of snow fell, basically south of a line from Shallow Water in southeast Scott County to Brownell in northeast Ness County to Pfeifer in southeast Ellis County, as well as north of a line from Elkhart in southeast Morton County to near Ashland in Clark county to near Sawyer in southeastern Pratt County.  In northern parts of DDC���s CWA, as well as in southeastern parts, 2 to 4 inches of snow fell.  The highest snowfall report received from this event was 11 inches at Ingalls in Gray County, with drifts as high as 5 feet in the Dodge City area also reported.  Winds during the event were from the north to northwest at 20 to 35 mph, which caused many areas of deep drifting and widespread travel problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 inches was reported with drifts to 5 feet from a location 1 mile north of Dodge City.  The snow was still falling at the time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two storm systems merged across the area on February 7th and 8th, 2010. The snow fell over an extended period of time, and winter storm and heavy snow criteria were not met. However, up to 5.7 inches of snow was reported in Centerville Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Centerville Kansas had 5.7 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system, brought snow to the region on February 4th and 5th, 2010. The heaviest snows fell across extreme east central Kansas. Accumulations of 4 to 8.5 inches was observed in this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 8.5 inches of snow was observed across southern portions of the county.

At the surface, several waves of precipitation moved through the area, with periodic sleet, snow, and rain showers, persisting from late Saturday afternoon through daybreak Sunday. However, the heaviest precipitation was still to come. ||Late on the 21st, a large area of thunderstorms developed in Texas, Oklahoma, and Kansas and began streaming northeast towards the area. While thunderstorm activity diminished as precipitation arrived, heavy rainfall still took place across portions of central Missouri. Numerous streams, rivers, and creeks went above flood stage. This precipitation area also produced rapid ice accumulations, south of the Missouri River. ||However, this was only the beginning of the heavy precipitation. As an area of surface low pressure and upper level low passed just south of the area, a large but incredibly intense band of snowfall, developed in areas of northern Kansas and northern Missouri on the morning of the 21st. This band of snow persisted through the evening hours of the 21st, producing snowfall rates between 1 and 2 inches per hour at times. Snowfall tapered off by the 22nd, as the upper level storm system moved well east of the region. Snowfall totals reached a foot in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 6 inches of snow was reported across the county. Blowing and drifting snow caused hazardous driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance produced scattered show showers across the area, during the afternoon of February 14th. While not uncommon to experience snow showers of this nature in the winter season, due to the extreme cold pocket of air above the ground and the very intense upward lift, these snow showers were able to produce an incredibly high volume of snow in a short amount of time. Making matters worse, were surface winds gusting to 35 mph at times.  These conditions created near whiteout visibilities, leading to multi-vehicle crashes. One such crash was located on Interstate 70, near Bonner Springs, and involved around 40 vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snow squall reduced visibilities to near zero on I-70. A 40 car pileup was reported near Bonner Springs around 1:30 pm with minor injuries (indirect).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved out of the Rockies and across the state of Kansas on Sunday, February 21.  As this storm system approached from the southwest, a cold airmass with sub freezing temperatures had already moved south across much of the area.  This provided the setup for a variety of wintry precipitation types as warm moist air moved north over the cold air at the surface.  Across east central Kansas a mix of freezing rain, sleet and snow fell with some areas accumulating more than a 1/2 inch of ice on trees and power lines.  A little further north of the ice, a swath of snow ranging from 5 to 8 inches fell across areas from Wabaunsee County through Shawnee, Jackson, and Jefferson Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm bringing periods of moderate to heavy snow moved into Jefferson county on February 20.  Reports from across the area indicated that between 4 to 9 inches of snow fell between February 20 and February 21.  The highest total from Jefferson county came out of Ozawkie, Kansas where 9 inches fell.  Blowing and drifting snow made travel through the area difficult.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant storm system moved across Oklahoma on February 7th and 8th, 2010.  An abundant area of moisture streamed to the north of this storm system into portions of Southern Kansas.  This moisture set up a narrow band of intense snowfall over portions of South Central Kansas from Kingman, Kansas to Wichita, Kansas to El Dorado, Kansas to north of Eureka, Kansas.  This band of snow led to snowfall amounts of 3 to 6 inches. The heavy wet snow led to trees sagging under the weight of the snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy wet snow blanketed the north side of Wichita with 4 to 6 inches of snowfall.  The wet snow coated trees and piled up on decks and outdoor furniture.  Most of the snowfall fell on elevated surfaces and grassy areas, but very little fell on roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several strong thunderstorms developed over extreme southeast Kansas and moved northeast into central Missouri. The storms produced hail up to nickle size along the track of the storms.

A stationary boundary oriented west to east set up across central Kansas.  During the morning hours the boundary lifted northward as a warm front and brought a period of severe weather characterized by marginally severe hail.  Later in the day storms initiated again along the surging cold front moving in from the west. Again, marginally severe hail was observed with these storms.
Thunderstorms from a slow moving storm system moved repeatedly over the same area leading to flooding and flash flooding across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clem Road was flooded with 8 inches of water over the road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were flooded due to creeks out of their banks across the county.

Snow accumulations of 4 to 10 inches west of the Highway 65 corridor with locally higher accumulations near one foot in southeast Kansas and west central Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow accumulations of up to one foot fell in the Fort Scott area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms moved along the Highway 54 corridor across Kansas and Missouri. The storms produced very heavy rainfall with rainfall totals exceeding 4.50 inches at some locations. The heavy rainfall, lead to significant flooding of low water crossings, small streams and urban areas. Hail to the size of ping pong balls and winds up to 60 mph were also reported with the storms as they moved across the Ozarks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A bridge was reported washed out, one mile south of Garland near the intersection of 230th and Deer roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front swept through the area scattered thunderstorms formed across east central Kansas.  Ahead of the front severe thunderstorms formed and dropped quarter sized hail in Lawrence and Ottawa.  Strong winds were also produced by these storms and caused some damage to trees and structures in Coffey and Anderson Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of a roof was blown off a house and several outbuildings were destroyed near the intersection of Arkansas Street and 900 Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary oriented west to east set up across central Kansas.  During the morning hours the boundary lifted northward as a warm front and brought a period of severe weather characterized by marginally severe hail.  Later in the day storms initiated again along the surging cold front moving in from the west. Again, marginally severe hail was observed with these storms.
A strong thunderstorm produced penny sized hail across Wyandotte County, during the early morning hours of April 5, 2010.

On the afternoon of April 29th, 2010 strong to severe thunderstorms formed across northern Kansas bringing large hail to the size of golfballs and damaging winds over 60 mph. A brief tornado touchdown occurred over open country approximately 4 miles northeast of Washington.  No condensation funnel appeared to reach the ground however dust was noted beneath the rotating wall cloud indicating a ground circulation on two occasions for a period of about 30 seconds.  No damage was found to have occurred.  The tornado was documented by the general public and storm chasers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Manhattan ASOS measured a 61 mph wind gust associated with strong gradient winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense late winter/early spring winter storm spread wintry precipitation and heavy snow across much of southeast Kansas from the evening hours of the 19th through the morning hours of the 21st. Accumulations were heaviest generally southeast of the Kansas Turnpike, ranging from 5 to 9 inches. Lighter accumulations ranging from 1 to 4 inches occurred generally northwest of the turnpike. Precipitation started out as a brief period of sleet and freezing rain during the late afternoon and evening hours on the 19th, especially across south-central, east-central and southeast Kansas. Numerous auto accidents occurred across the region due to slick roads, some of which included injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 2 to 4 inches across Lincoln County, with 4 inches at Barnard. Numerous traffic accidents occurred county-wide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure area moved along the Kansas-Oklahoma border during the afternoon and evening of March 10th, 2010.  As this low moved along, low topped thunderstorms developed over portions of Southeast Kansas over the Flint Hills. The small thunderstorms produced a swath of hail as they moved northeast, even producing enough hail to cover the ground in some locations. The hail also produced some piles along Highway 54 in Greenwood County, Kansas.

A strong upper level low pressure area moved along the Kansas-Oklahoma border during the afternoon and evening of March 10th, 2010.  As this low moved along, low topped thunderstorms developed over portions of Southeast Kansas over the Flint Hills. The small thunderstorms produced a swath of hail as they moved northeast, even producing enough hail to cover the ground in some locations. The hail also produced some piles along Highway 54 in Greenwood County, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to nickel sized hail covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical airmass associated with the remnants of Tropical Storm Alex moved into the central plains during the Fourth of July weekend.  A strong upper level low pressure system allowed numerous storms to form across the area. As a result several areas reported heavy rain and flooding.  Two tornadoes were reported during the afternoon of the 4th.  The first tornado formed near Mayetta, and crossed US 75 overturning a vehicle and travel trailer. It also hit a house near Mayetta causing some minor structural  damage. A second tornado formed briefly in southern Shawnee county, but caused no damage and dissipated within seconds of forming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado touched down in extreme southern Shawnee County on the evening of July 4th. The tornado lasted less than 1 minute and caused only minor damage.  The tops of some trees were damaged, as was a tin outbuilding near Richland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms tracked across much of the northern half of Kansas during the afternoon and evening hours on Thursday, May 6 before moving out of the area during the early morning hours Friday, May 7.  Two swaths of strong winds and large hail were caused by the storms Thursday night.  A southern swath of severe weather was caused by a supercell which moved through Dickinson, Morris, Lyon, and Osage counties.  Hail up to the size of baseballs was reported as well as strong winds up to 90 to 100 mph.  A house and its attached garage near Hope, KS were seriously damaged as a result of the strong winds just south of the supercell.  Other outbuildings were also destroyed near Hope.||Another swath of more widespread severe weather presented itself just south of the Kansas/Nebraska border as numerous supercells moved eastward through north central and northeastern Kansas.  Hail up to the size of baseballs was reported throughout much of the area during the late evening to overnight hours. Strong 80 mph winds caused a large amount of damage to outbuildings throughout Republic county.  Cars were also crushed under large trees which were snapped by the strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm bore down on western Kansas Sunday and Monday, February 7th and 8th, 2010.  The upper level storm brought moisture and unsettled weather to southwestern Kansas, and was then followed by a strong arctic cold front blasting south through the area Sunday night.  This front brought cold air and strong north winds of 15 to 30 mph with it, which caused some blowing and drifting snow problems.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 4inches within a 30 to 40 mile wide swath running from northeastern Haskell County eastward to the eastern part of Pratt County.   A narrow 10 to 15 mile wide band of around 2 inches fell across the rest of southwestern Kansas, basically south of a line from Scott City in Scott County to east of Lacrosse in Rush County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch of snow fell in Greensburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong southerly winds developed during the mid to late evening hours of the 29th across portions of mainly Central Kansas. Sustained speeds around 40 mph were common, with localized gusts of 50 to 63 mph. The unusually strong southerly winds were likely caused by 80 to 100 mph southerly winds (low-level jet) around 2,000 feet above the ground. Increased inflow into strong to severe thunderstorms approaching from the northwest also likely aided the strong winds. The winds subsided some around midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured by AWOS equipment at the McPherson Airport. Measured sustained speeds ranged from 40 to 43 mph, while measured gusts ranged from 49 to 54 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured by the Russell Airport ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms moved along the Highway 54 corridor across Kansas and Missouri. The storms produced very heavy rainfall with rainfall totals exceeding 4.50 inches at some locations. The heavy rainfall, lead to significant flooding of low water crossings, small streams and urban areas. Hail to the size of ping pong balls and winds up to 60 mph were also reported with the storms as they moved across the Ozarks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of 3rd Street and Highway 69 in town was flooded and impassable to motorists.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of April 29th, 2010 strong to severe thunderstorms formed across northern Kansas bringing large hail to the size of golfballs and damaging winds over 60 mph. A brief tornado touchdown occurred over open country approximately 4 miles northeast of Washington.  No condensation funnel appeared to reach the ground however dust was noted beneath the rotating wall cloud indicating a ground circulation on two occasions for a period of about 30 seconds.  No damage was found to have occurred.  The tornado was documented by the general public and storm chasers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado touchdown occurred over open country approximately 4 miles northeast of Washington.  No condensation funnel appeared to reach the ground however dust was noted beneath the rotating wall cloud indicating a ground circulation on two occasions for a period of about 30 seconds.  No damage was found to have occurred.  The tornado was documented by the general public and storm chasers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several slow-moving supercells developed in extreme eastern Colorado and moved across Greeley, Wichita, Logan and Gove counties, producing five tornadoes and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado was described as very large.  Approximately 15 power poles were damaged on Road L associated with this tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms tracked across much of the northern half of Kansas during the afternoon and evening hours on Thursday, May 6 before moving out of the area during the early morning hours Friday, May 7.  Two swaths of strong winds and large hail were caused by the storms Thursday night.  A southern swath of severe weather was caused by a supercell which moved through Dickinson, Morris, Lyon, and Osage counties.  Hail up to the size of baseballs was reported as well as strong winds up to 90 to 100 mph.  A house and its attached garage near Hope, KS were seriously damaged as a result of the strong winds just south of the supercell.  Other outbuildings were also destroyed near Hope.||Another swath of more widespread severe weather presented itself just south of the Kansas/Nebraska border as numerous supercells moved eastward through north central and northeastern Kansas.  Hail up to the size of baseballs was reported throughout much of the area during the late evening to overnight hours. Strong 80 mph winds caused a large amount of damage to outbuildings throughout Republic county.  Cars were also crushed under large trees which were snapped by the strong winds.
