Featuring both a morning and evening round of severe thunderstorms, Saturday the 19th was one of the most active severe weather days of 2010 across parts of North Central Kansas. The first round of severe storms, in the form of a MCS, rolled from west to east across the area within a few hours after sunrise. This complex of storms first organized across Northeast Colorado and Southwest Nebraska shortly after midnight, before rolling east-southeast into North Central Kansas. These storms were slightly elevated, focusing along the nose of a low level jet, and slightly north of a nearly stationary, east-west surface front draped across Central Kansas. The synoptic environment featured considerable instability, with MUCAPE values of 3000-4000 J/kg, and deep-layer wind shear of 30-40 knots. These storms produced both large hail and damaging winds, with hail up to golf ball size reported in Phillips County, and wind gusts to around 65 MPH in both Phillips and Mitchell Counties. In the Cawker City and Glen Elder areas, there was tree damage and significant structural damage to at least one mobile home. ||After a lull in the action that lasted most of the day, the second round of severe weather flared up during the late afternoon, lasting well into the evening hours. These storms focused directly along the east-west surface front, which had moved north during the day in response to deepening low pressure across the Central High Plains. Around storm initiation time, the environment was again very supportive of significant severe weather, with MLCAPE values around 4000 J/kg, and 40 knots of deep-layer wind shear. In addition, things were also becoming increasingly favorable for tornadoes, with 0-1 km storm-relative helicity climbing into the 200-300 m2/s2 range, and 0-1 km bulk shear of 20-25 knots. Given these parameters, storms started out as semi-discrete supercells, the strongest of which produced an EF1 tornado near Randall in Jewell County that damaged a farmstead. Although this was the only tornado reported during the evening, there were numerous reports of large hail, high winds and flooding rainfall as the initial storms merged into a nearly stationary east-west line that remained draped over parts of the area for several hours. Flash flooding resulted in water over several roads, with radar estimations of at least 5-7 inches of rain across parts of southern Phillips County and adjacent areas. The Bow Creek near the Phillips and Rooks County line rose more than 5 feet above flood stage, setting an all-time record crest of 14.29 feet.

The remnants of a weak tropical circulation over Louisiana spread deep moisture into the Ozarks. This interacted with a stalled front and numerous outflow boundaries which produced persistent, heavy rain producing, thunderstorms across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several power poles were downed near the intersection of 140th Street and Jayhawk Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early to mid morning hours on this Wednesday, a persistent area of showers and thunderstorms dumped very heavy rainfall across parts of North Central Kansas, resulting in fairly widespread flooding. Per gauge readings, the heaviest rain of at least 2-5 inches targeted southern Smith, northeast Osborne, and much of Mitchell Counties. This heavy rain event was driven by a 25-35 knot low level jet impinging upon a very moist airmass featuring precipitatable water values around 2 inches. With most of this rain inundating the North Fork Solomon River basin, flooding was imminent, and the North Fork Solomon at Portis rapidly rose to over 7 feet above flood stage by the noon hour. Elsewhere in the area, water was reported over Highway 24 near Glen Elder for a short time, and a road near Harlan in far southern Smith County was washed out along a 60-foot wide stretch, with water 20-feet deep. This prevented a rural Harlan family from returning home that day, with one of the residents reporting storm total rainfall in excess of 7 inches.

Over the course of 3 hours on this late Wednesday afternoon and evening, a nearly solid line of severe thunderstorms lumbered slowly east-southeast across parts of North Central Kansas, primarily affecting areas east of a line from Smith Center to Plainville. These storms developed along a well-defined, advancing cold front, in a very|unstable environment characterized by 3500-5000 J/kg MLCAPE, but only around 30 knots of deep-layer vertical wind shear. In the mid and upper levels, a shortwave trough centered to the north over the Dakotas served as the primary instigator for severe weather. These storms resulted in several reports of damaging wind gusts of 60-70 MPH, mainly across Osborne and Mitchell Counties, along with a few hail reports ranging from penny to quarter size.

As is typical on the 4th of July in western Kansas, thunderstorms disrupted festivities at many locations. Excessive rainfall occurred at a few spots.  A shortwave trough moved across a tropical air mass that was in place.  Flooding that resulted continued into the 5h.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was flowing across highway 2 west of Kiowa as runoff from heavy rainfall persisted for a while.

These storms were slightly elevated, focusing along the nose of a low level jet, and slightly north of a nearly stationary, east-west surface front draped across Central Kansas. The synoptic environment featured considerable instability, with MUCAPE values of 3000-4000 J/kg, and deep-layer wind shear of 30-40 knots. These storms produced both large hail and damaging winds, with hail up to golf ball size reported in Phillips County, and wind gusts to around 65 MPH in both Phillips and Mitchell Counties. In the Cawker City and Glen Elder areas, there was tree damage and significant structural damage to at least one mobile home. ||After a lull in the action that lasted most of the day, the second round of severe weather flared up during the late afternoon, lasting well into the evening hours. These storms focused directly along the east-west surface front, which had moved north during the day in response to deepening low pressure across the Central High Plains. Around storm initiation time, the environment was again very supportive of significant severe weather, with MLCAPE values around 4000 J/kg, and 40 knots of deep-layer wind shear. In addition, things were also becoming increasingly favorable for tornadoes, with 0-1 km storm-relative helicity climbing into the 200-300 m2/s2 range, and 0-1 km bulk shear of 20-25 knots. Given these parameters, storms started out as semi-discrete supercells, the strongest of which produced an EF1 tornado near Randall in Jewell County that damaged a farmstead. Although this was the only tornado reported during the evening, there were numerous reports of large hail, high winds and flooding rainfall as the initial storms merged into a nearly stationary east-west line that remained draped over parts of the area for several hours. Flash flooding resulted in water over several roads, with radar estimations of at least 5-7 inches of rain across parts of southern Phillips County and adjacent areas. The Bow Creek near the Phillips and Rooks County line rose more than 5 feet above flood stage, setting an all-time record crest of 14.29 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

In addition, things were also becoming increasingly favorable for tornadoes, with 0-1 km storm-relative helicity climbing into the 200-300 m2/s2 range, and 0-1 km bulk shear of 20-25 knots. Given these parameters, storms started out as semi-discrete supercells, the strongest of which produced an EF1 tornado near Randall in Jewell County that damaged a farmstead. Although this was the only tornado reported during the evening, there were numerous reports of large hail, high winds and flooding rainfall as the initial storms merged into a nearly stationary east-west line that remained draped over parts of the area for several hours. Flash flooding resulted in water over several roads, with radar estimations of at least 5-7 inches of rain across parts of southern Phillips County and adjacent areas. The Bow Creek near the Phillips and Rooks County line rose more than 5 feet above flood stage, setting an all-time record crest of 14.29 feet.

During the early to mid morning hours, elevated thunderstorms developed across North Central Kansas on the nose of a southwesterly 35 to 40 knot low level jet. In the presence of 40 knots of deep layer shear, steep mid-level lapse rates, and around 2000 J/kg of MUCAPE, one of these storms became severe as it drifted southeast across Rooks County. This storm was primarily a hail producer, with hail up to golf ball size reported along a narrow corridor near Webster State Park.

A powerful storm system moved across northern Kansas on Saturday morning, producing winds of 60 to 80 mph or greater from north central Kansas well into Missouri.  Some of the strongest winds were experienced along a swath extending from Concordia through Barnes, Blue Rapids and Vermillion along Highway 9, and between highway 16 and 36 over extreme northeast Kansas. Later that evening another round of severe weather brought damaging winds, large hail, and flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Norway reported a 64 mph wind gust. In Agenda power lines were damaged and large trees snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June 19, 2010 was not finished, as another bow echo passed along or near the same areas, as convection from earlier in the day. Winds were not as strong as the earlier convection, and the bow weakened as it moved into Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds gusted to an estimated 65 mph. Numerous trees were reported down in the city, blocking roads and causing power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous strong to severe thunderstorms affected portions of south-central, east-central and southeast Kansas during the late afternoon and evening hours on the 24th, as a cold front approached from the northwest. Hail up to quarter size, winds to 65 mph and locally heavy rainfall were the main weather risks. The locally heavy rainfall did produce some localized minor flooding of typical flood-prone and poor drainage areas across portions of Labette, Chase, Allen, Butler and Woodson counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Occurred at the intersection of 1st Street and Oliver.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous strong to severe thunderstorms affected portions of central and east-central Kansas from the late afternoon hours on the 20th through the early morning hours on the 21st. The thunderstorms developed along and north of a warm front extending west to east across Kansas. Damaging winds up to 80 mph and hail to the size of ping pong balls occurred, along with pockets of very heavy rainfall. The locally heavy rainfall did produce some localized minor flooding of typical flood-prone and poor drainage areas across portions of Barton county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated at 60 mph blew over a camper near mile marker 248 on Interstate 70.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Over the course of 3 hours on this late Wednesday afternoon and evening, a nearly solid line of severe thunderstorms lumbered slowly east-southeast across parts of North Central Kansas, primarily affecting areas east of a line from Smith Center to Plainville. These storms developed along a well-defined, advancing cold front, in a very|unstable environment characterized by 3500-5000 J/kg MLCAPE, but only around 30 knots of deep-layer vertical wind shear. In the mid and upper levels, a shortwave trough centered to the north over the Dakotas served as the primary instigator for severe weather. These storms resulted in several reports of damaging wind gusts of 60-70 MPH, mainly across Osborne and Mitchell Counties, along with a few hail reports ranging from penny to quarter size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture left over from the remnants of Tropical Storm Alex streamed north and interacted with strong upper level dynamics to produce widespread heavy rainfall across Kansas over a three day period.  This rainfall event started over the Fourth of July holiday weekend, washing out alot of the festivities associated with the holiday.   This heavy rainfall event continued until July 7th, 2010.  Numerous rounds of heavy rainfall led to countywide flooding across numerous counties, with some fields and low lying areas remaining flooded for the whole week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts were 4.60 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the pre-dawn hours on this Saturday, a northwest to southeast oriented corridor of strong to severe thunderstorms developed across parts of North Central Kansas, resulting in locally heavy rainfall and a few reports of nickel to quarter size hail.  These storms initially flared up around midnight, along a rather subtle low to mid level convergence zone, in a very unstable airmass featuring 4000-5000 J/kg most-unstable CAPE and 30-40 knots of deep-layer vertical wind shear. Although storms underwent a general weakening trend through the night, they persisted across parts of the area through sunrise. Some of the heaviest rainfall targeted Smith County, where the NWS cooperative observer in Lebanon reported an overnight total of 3.15 inches.

A tropical airmass associated with the remnants of Tropical Storm Alex moved into the central plains during the Fourth of July weekend.  A strong upper level low pressure system allowed numerous storms to form across the area. As a result several areas reported heavy rain and flooding.  Two tornadoes were reported during the afternoon of the 4th.  The first tornado formed near Mayetta, and crossed US 75 overturning a vehicle and travel trailer. It also hit a house near Mayetta causing some minor structural  damage. A second tornado formed briefly in southern Shawnee county, but caused no damage and dissipated within seconds of forming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches of water was running over Mulberry Road near Ada.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of hot daytime temperatures, unseasonably high humidity levels and sultry nighttime temperatures resulted in an oppressive heat wave across the region from the 16th through the 24th. Afternoon heat index values ranged from 105-112 degrees each day. The combination of the heat, humidity and relatively warm nighttime temperatures killed over 2600 cattle in up to 10 feedlots throughout Central and Southwest Kansas, mainly from the 15th through the 18th. A Great Bend facility alone lost over 400 cattle. Taking into account cattle deaths and weight loss of remaining cattle, monetary losses from central to southwest Kansas were likely around $7 million, with roughly $3.5 million of those losses occurring across central Kansas, including portions of Barton, Rice, Reno, Ellsworth and Saline counties. Unfortunately, cattle numbers for specific feedlots are unknown. The following newspapers contributed to this report: Great Bend Tribune, Salina Journal, Hutchinson News, Chanute Tribune, Coffeyville Journal and the Winfield Daily Courier.

For the second consecutive late afternoon and evening, a complex of severe thunderstorms rolled across parts of North Central Kansas on this Sunday, resulting in several reports of damaging winds, and even an isolated, brief tornado. Again, these storms focused almost directly along a nearly stationary, east-west surface front stretched all the way across Northern Kansas. The initial storm development took place across Northwest Kansas at mid-afternoon, as a subtle mid level shortwave ejected into the region from a large-scale trough anchored over the western United States.  As storms spread east into North Central Kansas in the form of a squall line, the synoptic environment was very supportive of severe weather, featuring deep-layer wind shear of 40-50 knots, and MLCAPE values around 4500 J/kg. Over the course of about two hours, there were numerous reports of 60-70 MPH wind gusts, and an estimated 75 MPH gust in Jewell County. Near the Smith and Jewell County line near Esbon, a brief EF0 tornado was reported in association with a bookend vortex that developed on the north side of a bowing segment in the squall line. While not widespread, there were a few reports of hail up to quarter size as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Featuring both a morning and evening round of severe thunderstorms, Saturday the 19th was one of the most active severe weather days of 2010 across parts of North Central Kansas. The first round of severe storms, in the form of a MCS, rolled from west to east across the area within a few hours after sunrise. This complex of storms first organized across Northeast Colorado and Southwest Nebraska shortly after midnight, before rolling east-southeast into North Central Kansas. These storms were slightly elevated, focusing along the nose of a low level jet, and slightly north of a nearly stationary, east-west surface front draped across Central Kansas. The synoptic environment featured considerable instability, with MUCAPE values of 3000-4000 J/kg, and deep-layer wind shear of 30-40 knots. These storms produced both large hail and damaging winds, with hail up to golf ball size reported in Phillips County, and wind gusts to around 65 MPH in both Phillips and Mitchell Counties. In the Cawker City and Glen Elder areas, there was tree damage and significant structural damage to at least one mobile home. ||After a lull in the action that lasted most of the day, the second round of severe weather flared up during the late afternoon, lasting well into the evening hours. These storms focused directly along the east-west surface front, which had moved north during the day in response to deepening low pressure across the Central High Plains. Around storm initiation time, the environment was again very supportive of significant severe weather, with MLCAPE values around 4000 J/kg, and 40 knots of deep-layer wind shear. In addition, things were also becoming increasingly favorable for tornadoes, with 0-1 km storm-relative helicity climbing into the 200-300 m2/s2 range, and 0-1 km bulk shear of 20-25 knots. Given these parameters, storms started out as semi-discrete supercells, the strongest of which produced an EF1 tornado near Randall in Jewell County that damaged a farmstead. Although this was the only tornado reported during the evening, there were numerous reports of large hail, high winds and flooding rainfall as the initial storms merged into a nearly stationary east-west line that remained draped over parts of the area for several hours. Flash flooding resulted in water over several roads, with radar estimations of at least 5-7 inches of rain across parts of southern Phillips County and adjacent areas. The Bow Creek near the Phillips and Rooks County line rose more than 5 feet above flood stage, setting an all-time record crest of 14.29 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second consecutive late afternoon and evening, a complex of severe thunderstorms rolled across parts of North Central Kansas on this Sunday, resulting in several reports of damaging winds, and even an isolated, brief tornado. Again, these storms focused almost directly along a nearly stationary, east-west surface front stretched all the way across Northern Kansas. The initial storm development took place across Northwest Kansas at mid-afternoon, as a subtle mid level shortwave ejected into the region from a large-scale trough anchored over the western United States.  As storms spread east into North Central Kansas in the form of a squall line, the synoptic environment was very supportive of severe weather, featuring deep-layer wind shear of 40-50 knots, and MLCAPE values around 4500 J/kg. Over the course of about two hours, there were numerous reports of 60-70 MPH wind gusts, and an estimated 75 MPH gust in Jewell County. Near the Smith and Jewell County line near Esbon, a brief EF0 tornado was reported in association with a bookend vortex that developed on the north side of a bowing segment in the squall line. While not widespread, there were a few reports of hail up to quarter size as well.

A powerful storm system moved across northern Kansas on Saturday morning, producing winds of 60 to 80 mph or greater from north central Kansas well into Missouri. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous strong to severe thunderstorms affected portions of central and east-central Kansas from the late afternoon hours on the 20th through the early morning hours on the 21st. The thunderstorms developed along and north of a warm front extending west to east across Kansas. Damaging winds up to 80 mph and hail to the size of ping pong balls occurred, along with pockets of very heavy rainfall. The locally heavy rainfall did produce some localized minor flooding of typical flood-prone and poor drainage areas across portions of Barton county.

The combination of hot daytime temperatures, unseasonably high humidity levels and sultry nighttime temperatures resulted in an oppressive heat wave across the region from the 16th through the 24th. Afternoon heat index values ranged from 105-112 degrees each day. The combination of the heat, humidity and relatively warm nighttime temperatures killed over 2600 cattle in up to 10 feedlots throughout Central and Southwest Kansas, mainly from the 15th through the 18th. A Great Bend facility alone lost over 400 cattle. Taking into account cattle deaths and weight loss of remaining cattle, monetary losses from central to southwest Kansas were likely around $7 million, with roughly $3.5 million of those losses occurring across central Kansas, including portions of Barton, Rice, Reno, Ellsworth and Saline counties. Unfortunately, cattle numbers for specific feedlots are unknown. The following newspapers contributed to this report: Great Bend Tribune, Salina Journal, Hutchinson News, Chanute Tribune, Coffeyville Journal and the Winfield Daily Courier.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The oppressive heat and humidity killed an estimated 500 to 600 cattle in feedlots across Barton County. A Great Bend facility alone lost over 400 cattle. Monetary loss estimates include cattle deaths along with the 40 to 70 pounds of average weight loss most heads of cattle incurred.

During the early to mid evening hours, a complex of strong to severe thunderstorms flared up across Northwest Kansas, spreading east into North Central Kansas by late evening. These storms formed along a nearly stationary east-west surface front, and organized within the nose of a 30-40 knot low level jet focused into the area. The synoptic environment also featured deep-layer wind shear of 40-50 knots, and MUCAPE values of 2000-3000 J/kg. While the majority of severe weather reports focused north of the Kansas and Nebraska border, one storm in Phillips County produced 60 MPH winds near Phillipsburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early to mid morning hours, elevated thunderstorms developed across North Central Kansas along the eastern edge of a 30-40 knot low level jet, and the associated low-mid level warm air/moisture advection regime. Although MUCAPE values were around 2000 J/kg, deep-layer wind shear was quite weak. As a result, severe weather was not much of an issue, with only a report of penny size hail in northeast Osborne County.

A tropical airmass associated with the remnants of Tropical Storm Alex moved into the central plains during the Fourth of July weekend.  A strong upper level low pressure system allowed numerous storms to form across the area. As a result several areas reported heavy rain and flooding.  Two tornadoes were reported during the afternoon of the 4th.  The first tornado formed near Mayetta, and crossed US 75 overturning a vehicle and travel trailer. It also hit a house near Mayetta causing some minor structural  damage. A second tornado formed briefly in southern Shawnee county, but caused no damage and dissipated within seconds of forming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water over K4 Highway in several places was reported near and west of Herington.  Also, Union Road, north of Herington, and Highway 43, north of hope, had water over the roadway.

A stationary frontal boundary draped across Central Kansas helped lead to the development of showers and thunderstorms across the region late in the afternoon of June 12th, 2010.  Abundant moisture along this frontal boundary led to the development of supercell thunderstorms with a few weak tornadoes, which mainly touched down in open fields.  The thunderstorms evolved into a line of storms and eventually led to an extended period of heavy rainfall during the overnight hours of June 12th into the early morning hours on June 13th, with some widespread flooding in Central Kansas.  The areas hit the hardest with flooding were just to the north and northeast of Hutchinson, Kansas, and to the northeast of Newton, Kansas near Peabody, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado touched down near the intersection of Highway 96 and Northwest 20th road. Some very minor damage was done to a couple of outbuildings on a farmstead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture left over from the remnants of Tropical Storm Alex streamed north and interacted with strong upper level dynamics to produce widespread heavy rainfall across Kansas over a three day period.  This rainfall event started over the Fourth of July holiday weekend, washing out alot of the festivities associated with the holiday.   This heavy rainfall event continued until July 7th, 2010.  Numerous rounds of heavy rainfall led to countywide flooding across numerous counties, with some fields and low lying areas remaining flooded for the whole week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five inches of rainfall led to flooding in the town of Haven. Numerous basements were flooded. Some homes had up to 10 inches of water in the basement.

During the early to mid morning hours, elevated thunderstorms developed across North Central Kansas on the nose of a southwesterly 35 to 40 knot low level jet. This storm was primarily a hail producer, with hail up to golf ball size reported along a narrow corridor near Webster State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Over the course of 3 hours on this late Wednesday afternoon and evening, a nearly solid line of severe thunderstorms lumbered slowly east-southeast across parts of North Central Kansas, primarily affecting areas east of a line from Smith Center to Plainville. These storms developed along a well-defined, advancing cold front, in a very|unstable environment characterized by 3500-5000 J/kg MLCAPE, but only around 30 knots of deep-layer vertical wind shear. In the mid and upper levels, a shortwave trough centered to the north over the Dakotas served as the primary instigator for severe weather. These storms resulted in several reports of damaging wind gusts of 60-70 MPH, mainly across Osborne and Mitchell Counties, along with a few hail reports ranging from penny to quarter size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated at 70 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon hours on this Monday, a few supercell storms with large hail developed across Northwest Kansas, as a mid-level disturbance shifted into the region on west-northwest flow aloft. Within an environment featuring 2000-2500 J/kg MLCAPE and deep-layer vertical wind shear of at least 40 knots, some of these Northwest Kansas storms underwent left and right-moving splits, with one of the east-moving left splits making it into Phillips County by early evening. Although this storm was weakening considerably at it entered western portions of the county, it still managed to produce penny size hail west of Long Island.

A cluster of severe thunderstorms with locally heavy rainfall made for an active Friday evening across parts of North Central Kansas, especially in Phillips County. The initial severe storms developed during the mid to late evening hours in the vicinity of a quasi-stationary surface front draped from Northwest Kansas to East Central Nebraska. With surface dewpoints pushing the low 70s in many areas, the airmass was quite unstable as storms organized, with MLCAPE values into the 3000-4000 j/kg range. A few of these initial storms were supercells, resulting in reports of quarter sized hail and wind gusts in excess of 60 MPH in the Phillipsburg area. ||In addition, parts of northern Phillips County received heavy rainfall in excess of 2.5 inches, resulting in water flowing over a few county roads, which were closed for a brief period of time.

Later that evening another round of severe weather brought damaging winds, large hail, and flash flooding.

In the wake of morning thunderstorms, afternoon sunshine heated an already very humid airmass over west central Missouri and eastern Kansas on Tuesday, June 8 2010, creating a very unstable atmosphere over the region. A slow moving cold front reach a Wichita to Kansas City line by late afternoon, rapidly igniting a long line of strong, slow moving thunderstorms. While the threat of damaging winds and large hail were diminished by the vast number of storms that developed along the front, excessive rainfall quickly became a concern. Training, or repeated thunderstorms tracking over the same area, deluged portions of the Kansas City metro into Cass County. Areas from Overland Park to Grandview to Lee's Summit, southward to Peculiar, Pleasant Hill and adjacent communities were hit especially hard as two and a half to four inches of rain fell in just a couple hours. Interstate 435 near the Kansas/Missouri state line was turned into a river at rush hour, while water rescues were conducted near Cleveland, Missouri to the southeast. In addition to the torrential rainfall, these storms were prolific lightning producers, with nearly continuous cloud-to-ground lightning observed well into the early evening hours. National lightning detection network counts showed total cloud-to-ground lightning strikes well into the tens of thousands, but thankfully no damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inches of water was reported near State Line Road on I-435. Torrential rains were reported in the area.

Interstate 435 near the Kansas/Missouri state line was turned into a river at rush hour, while water rescues were conducted near Cleveland, Missouri to the southeast. In addition to the torrential rainfall, these storms were prolific lightning producers, with nearly continuous cloud-to-ground lightning observed well into the early evening hours. National lightning detection network counts showed total cloud-to-ground lightning strikes well into the tens of thousands, but thankfully no damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains caused water to reach three feet deep, around Mission Road and 127th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system moved across northern Kansas on Saturday morning, producing winds of 60 to 80 mph or greater from north central Kansas well into Missouri.  Some of the strongest winds were experienced along a swath extending from Concordia through Barnes, Blue Rapids and Vermillion along Highway 9, and between highway 16 and 36 over extreme northeast Kansas. Later that evening another round of severe weather brought damaging winds, large hail, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported an estimated 80 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical airmass associated with the remnants of Tropical Storm Alex moved into the central plains during the Fourth of July weekend.  A strong upper level low pressure system allowed numerous storms to form across the area. As a result several areas reported heavy rain and flooding.  Two tornadoes were reported during the afternoon of the 4th.  The first tornado formed near Mayetta, and crossed US 75 overturning a vehicle and travel trailer. It also hit a house near Mayetta causing some minor structural  damage. A second tornado formed briefly in southern Shawnee county, but caused no damage and dissipated within seconds of forming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of water was reported running over Highway 41, 3 miles east of Delphos. Also 120th road was under approximately 5 inches of water running out of nearby fields.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous strong to severe thunderstorms affected portions of central and east-central Kansas from the late afternoon hours on the 20th through the early morning hours on the 21st. The thunderstorms developed along and north of a warm front extending west to east across Kansas. Damaging winds up to 80 mph and hail to the size of ping pong balls occurred, along with pockets of very heavy rainfall. The locally heavy rainfall did produce some localized minor flooding of typical flood-prone and poor drainage areas across portions of Barton county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree limb was downed in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of hot daytime temperatures, unseasonably high humidity levels and sultry nighttime temperatures resulted in an oppressive heat wave across the region from the 16th through the 24th. Afternoon heat index values ranged from 105-112 degrees each day. The combination of the heat, humidity and relatively warm nighttime temperatures killed over 2600 cattle in up to 10 feedlots throughout Central and Southwest Kansas, mainly from the 15th through the 18th. A Great Bend facility alone lost over 400 cattle. Taking into account cattle deaths and weight loss of remaining cattle, monetary losses from central to southwest Kansas were likely around $7 million, with roughly $3.5 million of those losses occurring across central Kansas, including portions of Barton, Rice, Reno, Ellsworth and Saline counties. Unfortunately, cattle numbers for specific feedlots are unknown. The following newspapers contributed to this report: Great Bend Tribune, Salina Journal, Hutchinson News, Chanute Tribune, Coffeyville Journal and the Winfield Daily Courier.

A cluster of severe thunderstorms with locally heavy rainfall made for an active Friday evening across parts of North Central Kansas, especially in Phillips County. The initial severe storms developed during the mid to late evening hours in the vicinity of a quasi-stationary surface front draped from Northwest Kansas to East Central Nebraska. With surface dewpoints pushing the low 70s in many areas, the airmass was quite unstable as storms organized, with MLCAPE values into the 3000-4000 j/kg range. A few of these initial storms were supercells, resulting in reports of quarter sized hail and wind gusts in excess of 60 MPH in the Phillipsburg area. ||In addition, parts of northern Phillips County received heavy rainfall in excess of 2.5 inches, resulting in water flowing over a few county roads, which were closed for a brief period of time.

A stationary frontal boundary draped across Central Kansas helped lead to the development of showers and thunderstorms across the region late in the afternoon of June 12th, 2010.  Abundant moisture along this frontal boundary led to the development of supercell thunderstorms with a few weak tornadoes, which mainly touched down in open fields.  The thunderstorms evolved into a line of storms and eventually led to an extended period of heavy rainfall during the overnight hours of June 12th into the early morning hours on June 13th, with some widespread flooding in Central Kansas.  The areas hit the hardest with flooding were just to the north and northeast of Hutchinson, Kansas, and to the northeast of Newton, Kansas near Peabody, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The report was from a KSN-TV weather lab.

Thunderstorms developed in an elevated unstable atmosphere ahead of an advancing warm front during the overnight hours.  This activity continued until after daybreak as a weak upper level s/wv trough moved across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement estimated the wind at 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon and early evening hours, a narrow, broken line of severe thunderstorms formed along a stalling cold front across portions of North Central Kansas, primarily affecting areas near a corridor running from Smith Center through Kirwin and Palco.  These storms formed in a rather unstable environment featuring steep low-level lapse rates and 2000-3000 J/kg MLCAPE, but only 30-35 knots of deep layer wind shear due to the parent mid level shortwave trough swinging well to the north across the Dakotas and Minnesota. Hail and locally heavy rainfall were the primary features with these storms, with hail up to golf ball size reported southwest of Kirwin Reservoir.

A powerful storm system moved across northern Kansas on Saturday morning, producing winds of 60 to 80 mph or greater from north central Kansas well into Missouri.  Some of the strongest winds were experienced along a swath extending from Concordia through Barnes, Blue Rapids and Vermillion along Highway 9, and between highway 16 and 36 over extreme northeast Kansas. Later that evening another round of severe weather brought damaging winds, large hail, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters and law enforcement officers reported 60 mph wind gusts near Westmoreland and St. George. In Olsburg trees were reported blown down. KDOT was required to use chainsaws to move the trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small cluster of storms with a bow echo, moved east across eastern Kansas and northern Missouri, during the morning hours of June 19, 2010. This storm system packed a strong punch, with radar estimated velocities topping out in the 80 to 90 knot range at times.  While the majority of this air speed didn't translate down to the surface, numerous reports of 60, to as high as 70 mph winds, were common from Troy to Kansas City.  This storm system remained intact long enough to cross the entire state of Missouri, making it a tri-state complex.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were reported down with winds estimated to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of morning thunderstorms, afternoon sunshine heated an already very humid airmass over west central Missouri and eastern Kansas on Tuesday, June 8 2010, creating a very unstable atmosphere over the region. A slow moving cold front reach a Wichita to Kansas City line by late afternoon, rapidly igniting a long line of strong, slow moving thunderstorms. While the threat of damaging winds and large hail were diminished by the vast number of storms that developed along the front, excessive rainfall quickly became a concern. Training, or repeated thunderstorms tracking over the same area, deluged portions of the Kansas City metro into Cass County. Areas from Overland Park to Grandview to Lee's Summit, southward to Peculiar, Pleasant Hill and adjacent communities were hit especially hard as two and a half to four inches of rain fell in just a couple hours. Interstate 435 near the Kansas/Missouri state line was turned into a river at rush hour, while water rescues were conducted near Cleveland, Missouri to the southeast. In addition to the torrential rainfall, these storms were prolific lightning producers, with nearly continuous cloud-to-ground lightning observed well into the early evening hours. National lightning detection network counts showed total cloud-to-ground lightning strikes well into the tens of thousands, but thankfully no damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inches of water was reported flowing over the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of very hot weather occurred across Northeast Kansas between July 19th and July 23rd.  Daytime air temperatures reached between 98 and 106 while heat indices reached the 110 to 115 degree range.  Nighttime temperatures only fell into the 75 to 80 degree range through this period.  No fatalities were reported however hundreds of cattle did perish in Ottawa County due to the heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime air temperatures reached between 98 and 106 while heat indices reached the 110 to 115 degree range. Nighttime temperatures only fell into the 75 to 80 degree range through this period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture left over from the remnants of Tropical Storm Alex streamed north and interacted with strong upper level dynamics to produce widespread heavy rainfall across Kansas over a three day period.  This rainfall event started over the Fourth of July holiday weekend, washing out alot of the festivities associated with the holiday.   This heavy rainfall event continued until July 7th, 2010.  Numerous rounds of heavy rainfall led to countywide flooding across numerous counties, with some fields and low lying areas remaining flooded for the whole week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported over numerous roads mainly to the east of Sedan. County Road 32 north of highway 166 was barricaded due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Over the course of 3 hours on this late Wednesday afternoon and evening, a nearly solid line of severe thunderstorms lumbered slowly east-southeast across parts of North Central Kansas, primarily affecting areas east of a line from Smith Center to Plainville. These storms developed along a well-defined, advancing cold front, in a very|unstable environment characterized by 3500-5000 J/kg MLCAPE, but only around 30 knots of deep-layer vertical wind shear. In the mid and upper levels, a shortwave trough centered to the north over the Dakotas served as the primary instigator for severe weather. These storms resulted in several reports of damaging wind gusts of 60-70 MPH, mainly across Osborne and Mitchell Counties, along with a few hail reports ranging from penny to quarter size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs of 4 to 6 inches in diameter knocked down.

A cold front and associated upper level disturbance ignited strong to severe thunderstorms across central and southeast Kansas from the late afternoon into the mid-evening hours on the 31st. Winds of 60 to 80 mph were reported, along with quarter size hail. The strong winds produced some damage in the Luray area in Russell county. Additionally, localized minor street flooding was reported in Coffeyville due to heavy rainfall over a relatively short period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated 60 to 70 mph winds about 3 miles southwest of Cassoday.

This rainfall event started over the Fourth of July holiday weekend, washing out alot of the festivities associated with the holiday.   This heavy rainfall event continued until July 7th, 2010.  Numerous rounds of heavy rainfall led to countywide flooding across numerous counties, with some fields and low lying areas remaining flooded for the whole week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported over some county roads west of Iola. Nearly 3.20 inches of rainfall caused water to flow over Florida and County road 200 and Missouri and County Road 600.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of hot daytime temperatures, unseasonably high humidity levels and sultry nighttime temperatures resulted in an oppressive heat wave across the region from the 16th through the 24th. Afternoon heat index values ranged from 105-112 degrees each day. The combination of the heat, humidity and relatively warm nighttime temperatures killed over 2600 cattle in up to 10 feedlots throughout Central and Southwest Kansas, mainly from the 15th through the 18th. A Great Bend facility alone lost over 400 cattle. Taking into account cattle deaths and weight loss of remaining cattle, monetary losses from central to southwest Kansas were likely around $7 million, with roughly $3.5 million of those losses occurring across central Kansas, including portions of Barton, Rice, Reno, Ellsworth and Saline counties. Unfortunately, cattle numbers for specific feedlots are unknown. The following newspapers contributed to this report: Great Bend Tribune, Salina Journal, Hutchinson News, Chanute Tribune, Coffeyville Journal and the Winfield Daily Courier.

Oppressive heat and humidity for June standards affected central, south-central and southeast Kansas from the 20th through the 23rd. Afternoon heat indices from 103 to 107 degrees were common, highest generally east of the Kansas Turnpike over southeast Kansas. No reports of heat related illnesses, deaths, property or crop damages were received. A strong cold front moved southeast across the area late on the 23rd, ushering slightly cooler air southward across the area.

A cold front and associated upper level disturbance spread numerous showers and thunderstorms southeast across portions of mainly central and south-central Kansas, and portions of southeast Kansas generally along and east of the flint hills. The activity affected the region from the late evening hours on the 23rd through the early morning hours on the 24th. A handful of damaging wind reports were received across central Kansas during the late evening on the 23rd, with the activity transitioning to a heavy rainfall and localized flooding threat thereafter. Widespread rainfall amounts ranged from 1 to 3 inches, with pockets of 3 to 4 inches. The flooding was deemed relatively minor and localized, so specifics were not included in this report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds of 60 mph were estimated by a trained spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and associated upper level disturbance ignited strong to severe thunderstorms across central and southeast Kansas from the late afternoon into the mid-evening hours on the 31st. Winds of 60 to 80 mph were reported, along with quarter size hail. The strong winds produced some damage in the Luray area in Russell county. Additionally, localized minor street flooding was reported in Coffeyville due to heavy rainfall over a relatively short period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail occurred with 40 mph winds and torrential rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system moved across northern Kansas on Saturday morning, producing winds of 60 to 80 mph or greater from north central Kansas well into Missouri.  Some of the strongest winds were experienced along a swath extending from Concordia through Barnes, Blue Rapids and Vermillion along Highway 9, and between highway 16 and 36 over extreme northeast Kansas. Later that evening another round of severe weather brought damaging winds, large hail, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water flowed over Courtland Road at Highway 148. The city of Courtland also experienced flash flooding across streets in town. In Scandia Water flowed over the streets at Pawnee Road from Highway 36. Water flowed over streets on highway K148 at County 100 Road and Count 110 Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was across much of Highway 148 from Highway 81 eastward in Republic County. Several rural county roads in southern Republic County also had water across them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary frontal boundary draped across Central Kansas helped lead to the development of showers and thunderstorms across the region late in the afternoon of June 12th, 2010.  Abundant moisture along this frontal boundary led to the development of supercell thunderstorms with a few weak tornadoes, which mainly touched down in open fields.  The thunderstorms evolved into a line of storms and eventually led to an extended period of heavy rainfall during the overnight hours of June 12th into the early morning hours on June 13th, with some widespread flooding in Central Kansas.  The areas hit the hardest with flooding were just to the north and northeast of Hutchinson, Kansas, and to the northeast of Newton, Kansas near Peabody, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter also reported some minor street flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak stationary front situated across Southern Kansas and large amounts of low level moisture led to the development of severe thunderstorms across Southern Kansas.  The most significant storm was a high precipitation supercell that moved across portions of Southeast Kansas and produced widespread wind damage, due to microburst winds, along a 4 mile long path near Cherryvale, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Microburst winds estimated at 80 mph moved through town, knocking down several large trees.  Power was out across town, with seventeen homes experiencing some minor structural damage.  A barn east of town lost a portion of its roof.  The path of destruction was 2 miles wide by two miles long.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms affected portions of south-central, east-central and southeast Kansas during the late afternoon and early evening hours on the 16th.  The thunderstorms were in response to an upper level disturbance approaching from the northwest. A handful of reports were received, including quarter size hail and 60 mph winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail size ranged from nickel to quarter size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous strong to severe thunderstorms affected portions of central and east-central Kansas from the late afternoon hours on the 20th through the early morning hours on the 21st. The thunderstorms developed along and north of a warm front extending west to east across Kansas. Damaging winds up to 80 mph and hail to the size of ping pong balls occurred, along with pockets of very heavy rainfall. The locally heavy rainfall did produce some localized minor flooding of typical flood-prone and poor drainage areas across portions of Barton county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated at 80 mph downed 8 to 10 inch diameter tree branches, and snapped some power poles in half.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous strong to severe thunderstorms affected portions of south-central, east-central and southeast Kansas during the late afternoon and evening hours on the 24th, as a cold front approached from the northwest. Hail up to quarter size, winds to 65 mph and locally heavy rainfall were the main weather risks. The locally heavy rainfall did produce some localized minor flooding of typical flood-prone and poor drainage areas across portions of Labette, Chase, Allen, Butler and Woodson counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated at 60 mph carried a sheet metal shed about 25 to 50 yards, and also downed a 4 inch diameter tree limb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of very hot weather occurred across Northeast Kansas between July 19th and July 23rd.  Daytime air temperatures reached between 98 and 106 while heat indices reached the 110 to 115 degree range.  Nighttime temperatures only fell into the 75 to 80 degree range through this period.  No fatalities were reported however hundreds of cattle did perish in Ottawa County due to the heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several hundred head of cattle perished at a feedlot in Bennington, Kansas due to the heat.  The cattle refuse to eat and starved to death due to the heat.  Excessive heat warning criteria were not exceeded in this area however the prolonged nature of the heat and the conditions of the feedlot contributed to the losses.  The estimates of dollars losses are rough estimates based on the number of cattle lost and the estimated price per head of cattle.

Thunderstorms developed in an elevated unstable atmosphere ahead of an advancing warm front during the overnight hours.  This activity continued until after daybreak as a weak upper level s/wv trough moved across the region.

A strong upper level jet maxima helped to support isentropic lift across the area as a surface trough edged eastward.  Abundant moisture interacted with the features to produce widespread thunderstorms, some severe.
Severe weather commenced early in the afternoon as a very intense mid level potential vorticity anomaly and upward vertical motion overspread a very moist and unstable lower troposphere.
Isentropic lifting and warm air advection north of a warm front helped to generate elevated thunderstorms late in the evening and into the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail fell at the National Weather Service office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated heavy rain reports were received from Seward county from overnight thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.45 fell in about 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely intense shortwave trough moved into the central plains during the day and evening.  Even though the low levels of the atmosphere were relatively cool, thunderstorms developed explosively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three inch diameter tree branches were broken.  The spotter estimated winds of 60 to 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely intense shortwave trough moved into the central plains during the day and evening.  Even though the low levels of the atmosphere were relatively cool, thunderstorms developed explosively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were estimated to be between 55 and 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet maxima helped to support isentropic lift across the area as a surface trough edged eastward.  Abundant moisture interacted with the features to produce widespread thunderstorms, some severe.
Thunderstorms redeveloped during the overnight hours but began to dissipate during the morning of the 19th, but no before dumping heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.34 was reported during the early morning hours in Kismet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary front with upslope flow north of the front helped to produce thunderstorms in the evening hours.  Some became marginally severe.  There was one brief tornado reported in Pratt county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was so much hail that drifts of hail were still observed the following morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over extreme northwest Kansas and southwest Nebraska during the late afternoon hours.  Stronger storms produced quarter to golf ball sized hail through the evening hours.

Severe thunderstorms associated with a cold pool from an upper level low developed during the early morning hours of May 13th. The storms produced hail, winds up to 60 mph and flooding across portions of southeast Kansas. The heavy rainfall from the storms produced flooding across portions of the region which lasted into the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 60 mph wind gust occurred in a mainly rural area about a mile northeast of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain in northeast Kansas brought localized flooding to areas near Topeka. The hardest hit area was on the far southern end of town where rural county roads were covered by standing water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Morrill Road from 77th North to Montara Parkway has water over the road in two areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed across northeast Colorado into southwest Nebraska during the evening and moved southeast.  Several reports of damaging winds were received resulting in utility pole, tree and farm damage in the Burlington Colorado and Goodland Kansas areas.

Several isolated supercells moved south and east across Thomas, Decatur and Norton counties producing a long swath of large hail, some up to the size of grapefruits.  The storm which crossed Thomas county affected 35,000 acres of cropland.

During the late afternoon and early evening hours, a narrow, broken line of severe thunderstorms formed along a stalling cold front across portions of North Central Kansas, primarily affecting areas near a corridor running from Smith Center through Kirwin and Palco.  These storms formed in a rather unstable environment featuring steep low-level lapse rates and 2000-3000 J/kg MLCAPE, but only 30-35 knots of deep layer wind shear due to the parent mid level shortwave trough swinging well to the north across the Dakotas and Minnesota. Hail and locally heavy rainfall were the primary features with these storms, with hail up to golf ball size reported southwest of Kirwin Reservoir.

Serving as the culmination of a very active four days of severe weather, yet another round of severe thunderstorms rolled across parts of North Central Kansas during the early to mid evening hours. This cluster of storms initially formed in Northwest Kansas and moved east-northeast into the area, focused by the presence of a nearly stationary surface front near the Nebraska and Kansas border, along with deep layer wind shear values of 40-60 knots. Isolated instances of damaging wind were the primary features with these storms, with some tree limbs taken down at Agra in Phillips County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some tree limbs were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lone supercell formed in Sheridan county during the early evening and remained about stationary for over an hour, producing multiple tornadoes, hail and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extremely heavy rainfall occurred just west of Clayton in Norton county during the evening with radar estimated rainfall of 6 to 10 inches.  Law enforcement later that night reported that Prairie Dog Creek had gone out of its banks 4 miles east of Clayton sometime during the night, with locations of debris indicating a rapid water rise out of the banks.  Time of event is estimated from radar, rain gauge reports and law enforcement observations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper level disturbance sparked a handful of strong to severe thunderstorms across portions of central, south-central and southeast Kansas during the late afternoon and evening hours on the 16th. Some of the more notable severe weather reports include baseball size hail near Chetopa in Labette County, and a brief tornado touchdown near Penalosa in Kingman County.

Isolated severe thunderstorm activity brought damaging winds and marginally severe hail. The extent of the damage was confined to a few broken tree limbs and some minor roof damage near Meriden, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The automated weather station in Emporia reported a 60 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of intense thunderstorms moved southeast from Yuma county Colorado into northwest Kansas during the evening. One tornado developed in Yuma county, and a long path of large hail and damaging winds occurred over NW Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many reports of large hail were received from the St Francis area.  The emergency manager saved hail stones in his freezer which measured 3.5 inches in diameter the following day.  Car windshields were broken and some windows in houses were also broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening hours of May  10th, 2010, ahead of an approaching dry line and warm front.  This dynamic environment coupled with an unstable airmass led to the development of thunderstorms to the west and south of Wichita, Kansas with tornado producing supercells moving across portions of South Central Kansas. Two supercells in particular produced significant damage across portions of Kingman, Sedgwick and Cowley counties with some of the damage classified as EF-2 damage by survey teams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager reported the gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system moved across northern Kansas on Saturday morning, producing winds of 60 to 80 mph or greater from north central Kansas well into Missouri.  Some of the strongest winds were experienced along a swath extending from Concordia through Barnes, Blue Rapids and Vermillion along Highway 9, and between highway 16 and 36 over extreme northeast Kansas. Later that evening another round of severe weather brought damaging winds, large hail, and flash flooding.

A powerful storm system moved across northern Kansas on Saturday morning, producing winds of 60 to 80 mph or greater from north central Kansas well into Missouri.  Some of the strongest winds were experienced along a swath extending from Concordia through Barnes, Blue Rapids and Vermillion along Highway 9, and between highway 16 and 36 over extreme northeast Kansas. Later that evening another round of severe weather brought damaging winds, large hail, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters and Law enforcement officers reported 70 to 80 mph wind gusts across Washington County. In Washington numerous trees were uprooted, which brought down several power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Outflow boundaries and a continued very unstable atmosphere responded to an upper level s/wv trough to produce additional thunderstorms across the area.
An isolated thunderstorm produced 3/4 inch hail near Coldwater.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was mostly smaller than 3/4 but was accompanied by strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary, from the previous nights convection on June 12th, stalled over Southern Kansas, and provided the focus for renewed shower and thunderstorm development during the afternoon and evening hours of June 13th, 2010.  The storms initially had some large hail and damaging winds, but the training storms quickly evolved into a heavy rainfall and flooding event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several elm trees were knocked down, including one that was knocked down onto a garage.  The tree collapsed the garage and it was considered a total loss.
